proposals.title: Centrifuge Investigation of Liquefaction-Induced Deformations in Complex Topographies: From Level Ground to Compound Slopes
proposals.motivation: Current seismic design and liquefaction hazard maps often rely on simplified assumptions of level ground, despite many critical infrastructure sites (e.g., ports, embankment transitions, excavated basins) existing on or adjacent to slopes or complex geometries. Pre-2026 research, including the LEAP projects, has extensively validated models for uniform slopes, but the interaction between level ground, slope inclination, and man-made excavations remains poorly quantified. This gap limits our ability to predict differential settlements and lateral spreading for realistic site conditions.
proposals.hypothesis: The presence of even gentle slopes or adjacent excavated zones significantly alters the pore pressure generation, dissipation, and resultant deformation mechanisms in level ground deposits during seismic loading, challenging the assumption that level ground response can be analyzed in isolation.
proposals.method: We will conduct a series of dynamic centrifuge tests at Kyoto University, building on the LEAP-UCD-2017/2019 protocols. A new model container will be used to create compound geometries: a central level deposit of saturated Ottawa F-65 sand flanked by constructed slopes of varying inclinations (0° to 10°) and/or simulated excavated trenches. Instrumentation will include accelerometer arrays, pore pressure transducers (PPTs), and high-speed cameras for PIV analysis. Tests will subject these models to scaled earthquake motions of varying intensities, monitoring the spatial progression of liquefaction and deformation.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide a benchmark experimental dataset on liquefaction in complex topographies, quantifying the zone of influence where slopes affect nominally level ground. It will deliver improved guidelines for defining the 'level ground' assumption in practice and inform the development and validation of advanced numerical models for non-uniform site conditions.
proposals.novelty: While previous centrifuge studies have examined uniform slopes or level ground in isolation, this proposal systematically investigates the coupled response at their interface and in engineered cut/fill transitions, a critical yet understudied scenario for geotechnical earthquake engineering.

proposals.title: A Formal Validation Framework for Liquefaction Constitutive Models Using Multi-Facility Centrifuge Test Databases
proposals.motivation: The validation of numerical models for soil liquefaction remains ad-hoc and inconsistent, hindering their reliable use in geotechnical earthquake engineering practice. While collaborative projects like LEAP have generated valuable multi-facility centrifuge data, a standardized, quantitative process to objectively assess model performance against this benchmark data is lacking. This gap limits confidence in model predictions for critical infrastructure.
proposals.hypothesis: A formalized validation framework, based on quantitative metrics and statistical analysis of multi-facility experimental data, can objectively rank the performance of different constitutive models and identify their reliable ranges of application for liquefaction problems.
proposals.method: The framework will be developed by first curating a standardized database from LEAP-UCD-2017 and LEAP-ASIA-2019 centrifuge tests, focusing on key output variables (e.g., excess pore pressure, displacement). We will define a suite of quantitative error metrics (e.g., RMSE, normalized cross-correlation) and a scoring system. The core technical approach involves applying this framework to validate 2-3 widely used constitutive models (e.g., PM4Sand, SANISAND) via numerical simulation of the curated experiments. The final step will be a statistical synthesis of results to produce model performance classifications.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide the geotechnical community with a transparent, repeatable protocol for model validation, moving beyond qualitative comparisons. It will deliver a benchmarked assessment of specific constitutive models' capabilities and limitations for lateral spreading and sloping ground problems, directly informing engineering practice and future model development.
proposals.novelty: While multi-facility data exists, no pre-2026 study has proposed a formal, standardized scoring and classification system for liquefaction model validation that systematically incorporates data variability from different centrifuge facilities into the performance assessment.

proposals.title: Development of a Standardized, FAIR-Compliant Data Repository for Centrifuge Modeling of Soil Liquefaction and Seismic Slope Response
proposals.motivation: The validation and advancement of numerical models for geotechnical earthquake engineering, such as those for liquefaction-induced lateral spreading, rely critically on high-quality, well-documented experimental data. While collaborative projects like LEAP have generated invaluable centrifuge test data, these datasets are often dispersed, inconsistently formatted, and lack standardized metadata, hindering their full utility for future validation studies and the development of constitutive models. A centralized, structured archive is essential to maximize the return on investment from these complex and costly physical experiments.
proposals.hypothesis: By implementing a standardized, FAIR (Findable, Accessible, Interoperable, Reusable) data curation framework for centrifuge test data, we can significantly enhance the reproducibility of validation studies and enable more robust, data-driven advancements in numerical simulation of seismic soil behavior.
proposals.method: This project will establish a dedicated digital repository, building upon platforms like DesignSafe-CI, to archive datasets from key projects including LEAP-UCD-2017 and LEAP-ASIA-2019. We will develop and implement a standardized metadata schema, extending the existing NIST QuakeTables ontology, to describe soil properties (e.g., relative density, permeability), model geometry, sensor layouts, input motions, and all measured responses (accelerations, pore pressures, displacements). Data will be converted into open, non-proprietary formats (e.g., HDF5, JSON). A web interface will allow for structured querying, visualization of time-series data, and bulk download, with Digital Object Identifiers (DOIs) assigned to each curated experiment.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide the geotechnical earthquake engineering community with a vetted, easily accessible benchmark database, directly supporting the calibration and validation of next-generation constitutive models and numerical codes. It will establish a best-practice template for future experimental data sharing, promoting transparency and reproducibility in the field. The archive will serve as a critical resource for researchers worldwide, accelerating the cycle of physical testing, numerical validation, and model improvement.
proposals.novelty: While data from individual projects exist, this proposal systematically creates a unified, FAIR-compliant repository specifically tailored for the complex, multi-sensor, time-series data of geotechnical centrifuge testing, integrating disparate LEAP datasets with a comprehensive, discipline-specific metadata framework to maximize future usability.

proposals.title: Verification and Validation of a Coupled u-p Finite Element Liquefaction Model Using Formal Code Verification Techniques from Computational Fluid Dynamics
proposals.motivation: Numerical models for soil liquefaction are critical for seismic geotechnical engineering but are notoriously difficult to validate due to the complexity of soil-fluid interaction. Current validation often relies on qualitative comparison to centrifuge tests, lacking rigorous, quantitative procedures to separate numerical errors from model form errors. Formal verification methods from CFD, such as the Method of Manufactured Solutions (MMS), provide a mature framework to address this gap, ensuring the numerical implementation correctly solves the intended governing equations.
proposals.hypothesis: Applying formal code verification techniques from CFD to a coupled u-p finite element formulation for dynamic liquefaction analysis will enable the rigorous quantification of numerical error, leading to more reliable and trustworthy model predictions when validated against high-quality centrifuge experiments.
proposals.method: We will implement a standard u-p formulation for dynamic soil response in a research code. The core method is the Method of Manufactured Solutions (MMS): we will prescribe arbitrary, non-physical solutions for displacement and pore pressure, derive the corresponding analytical source terms, and insert them into the governing equations. The code will be run with these source terms, and the numerical output will be compared to the manufactured analytical solution to compute the order-of-accuracy. This process will be applied systematically to individual components (e.g., spatial discretization, time integration, coupling terms) using pre-2026 open-source libraries like FEniCS or deal.II for prototyping.
proposals.contribution: This work will produce a verified benchmark case for coupled liquefaction solvers, providing a clear standard for numerical correctness. It will deliver a procedural template for the geotechnical community to adopt rigorous V&V practices, moving beyond simple curve-matching. The resulting verified model will form a more reliable basis for subsequent validation against the LEAP centrifuge experimental database.
proposals.novelty: While MMS is standard in CFD, its application to the coupled, dynamic u-p equations governing soil liquefaction is novel and non-trivial due to the challenge of manufacturing solutions that satisfy the coupling constraints. This bridges a significant methodological gap between the advanced numerical practices of CFD and geotechnical engineering.

proposals.title: Full-Field Deformation Analysis of Liquefaction-Induced Lateral Spreading in Sloping Ground Using In-Flight Digital Image Correlation in Centrifuge Modeling
proposals.motivation: Understanding the full-field, transient deformation of soil during liquefaction-induced lateral spreading is critical for assessing and mitigating seismic risk to civil infrastructure. Current centrifuge model tests, including the LEAP projects, primarily rely on discrete sensor data (e.g., accelerometers, pore pressure transducers), which provide limited spatial resolution of soil displacement and strain localization. A high-resolution, full-field measurement technique is needed to validate advanced constitutive models and numerical simulations more rigorously.
proposals.hypothesis: The application of in-flight Digital Image Correlation (DIC) to centrifuge models of sloping ground will reveal previously unobserved patterns of strain localization and progressive failure mechanisms during seismic liquefaction, which are not fully captured by discrete instrumentation.
proposals.method: We will integrate a high-speed, high-resolution camera system into the Kyoto University geotechnical centrifuge to perform in-flight DIC on the sidewall of a transparent model container. A well-characterized, saturated sloping sand deposit, replicating LEAP-UCD-2017/2019 test conditions, will be prepared with a speckle pattern. Using established DIC algorithms (e.g., normalized cross-correlation or iterative least-squares matching), we will process the captured image sequences to compute 2D displacement and strain fields (ε_xx, ε_zz, γ_xz) throughout the seismic shaking event. The DIC data will be synchronized with traditional sensor measurements for comprehensive validation.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide the first high-resolution, full-field deformation dataset for a benchmark centrifuge liquefaction test, offering an unprecedented validation target for computational models. It will advance the mechanistic understanding of shear band formation and the evolution of lateral displacement in liquefiable slopes, directly informing the performance-based design of earth structures and foundations.
proposals.novelty: While DIC is established in experimental solid mechanics, its application to in-flight, dynamic centrifuge modeling of soil liquefaction—particularly for capturing transient, full-field deformations during seismic shaking—remains a significant technical challenge and is not standard practice in geotechnical engineering as of 2025.

proposals.title: Validation of Centrifuge-Derived Scaling Laws for Liquefaction-Induced Lateral Spreading via Large-Scale 1g Field Testing
proposals.motivation: The prediction of liquefaction-induced lateral spreading in sloping ground is critical for seismic risk assessment of infrastructure. While centrifuge modeling, such as the LEAP projects, has provided valuable data, the validation of its generalized scaling laws and the extrapolation of results to real-world, full-scale conditions remains a significant challenge. Direct large-scale field tests are essential to bridge this scale gap and improve the confidence in numerical and physical models used in engineering practice.
proposals.hypothesis: The generalized scaling laws validated in centrifuge experiments for liquefaction-induced lateral spreading will hold when applied to large-scale, 1g field tests, provided that soil fabric and stress history are appropriately accounted for. The mechanisms of deformation and pore pressure dissipation observed in centrifuges will be replicated at the field scale.
proposals.method: We will construct a large-scale (approx. 10m long, 5m wide, 3m deep), instrumented, sloping liquefiable sand deposit at a field testing site. The soil will be saturated and subjected to controlled, high-energy dynamic excitation using a large mobile shaker or explosive charges to induce liquefaction. The test will be densely instrumented with accelerometer arrays, pore pressure transducers, displacement sensors (LVDTs, inclinometers), and high-speed cameras to capture the full dynamic response. The measured accelerations, excess pore pressures, and permanent lateral displacements will be directly compared to predictions from scaled centrifuge model tests (e.g., LEAP-UCD-2017) using the generalized scaling law.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide a unique, high-quality dataset of full-scale liquefaction response, offering a definitive benchmark for validating centrifuge scaling laws and high-fidelity numerical models. It will directly address the uncertainty in extrapolating laboratory results to field conditions, leading to more reliable predictive tools for lateral spreading hazard assessment. The findings will inform revisions to scaling methodologies and improve the calibration of constitutive models used in practice.
proposals.novelty: While centrifuge tests are common, a purpose-built, fully instrumented, large-scale 1g field test designed explicitly to validate centrifuge-derived scaling laws for lateral spreading is a novel and critical step forward. It moves beyond comparative centrifuge studies (like LEAP-ASIA-2019) to a direct field-scale verification under realistic boundary conditions and soil volumes.

proposals.title: Calibration and Validation of a High-Fidelity Constitutive Model for Liquefiable Slopes Using Element-Level Cyclic Simple Shear Tests
proposals.motivation: The accurate prediction of liquefaction-induced lateral spreading in sloping ground remains a critical challenge in geotechnical earthquake engineering. While centrifuge tests, such as those in LEAP projects, provide invaluable system-level validation data, the constitutive models used in numerical simulations often lack rigorous calibration against high-quality element-level data. This gap limits the reliability and transferability of these models for new site conditions.
proposals.hypothesis: A constitutive model (specifically, the PM4Sand model, version 3.1) calibrated against a comprehensive suite of cyclic simple shear (CSS) tests on representative soil specimens will significantly improve the accuracy of numerical predictions for the dynamic response and permanent displacement of liquefiable slopes observed in centrifuge experiments.
proposals.method: We will perform a systematic series of stress-controlled cyclic simple shear tests on reconstituted specimens of Ottawa F-65 sand, matching the material used in LEAP-UCD-2017. Tests will cover a range of initial relative densities, confining stresses, and cyclic stress ratios (CSR). The resulting data on cyclic resistance, shear strain accumulation, and pore pressure generation will be used to calibrate the input parameters for the PM4Sand model (v3.1) within the FLAC or OpenSees framework. The calibrated model will then be used to simulate the LEAP-UCD-2017 centrifuge experiment, and the predicted accelerations, pore pressures, and lateral displacements will be quantitatively compared to the experimental benchmark.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide a publicly available, rigorously calibrated constitutive model dataset for a standard liquefiable sand, directly linked to a well-documented centrifuge validation case. It will establish a clear, reproducible methodology for bridging element-level laboratory data to boundary-value problem simulations, enhancing the credibility of numerical tools for seismic hazard assessment.
proposals.novelty: Unlike previous calibrations often based on limited test data or back-analysis of centrifuge results alone, this study explicitly uses a comprehensive, purpose-designed CSS test program as the sole calibration source, providing a pure test of the model's predictive capability when scaled up from element to system level.

proposals.title: A Unified Numerical Framework for Predicting Multi-Facility Centrifuge Test Results on Liquefiable Slopes: A LEAP-ASIA-2019 Benchmark Study
proposals.motivation: The LEAP (Liquefaction Experiments and Analysis Projects) initiative has demonstrated significant inter-facility variability in centrifuge test results for identical model specifications, highlighting a critical challenge in geotechnical physical modeling. This variability complicates the validation of numerical constitutive models, as a model calibrated to one facility's data may fail to predict another's. A robust, predictive numerical framework is therefore essential to decouple true soil behavior from facility-specific effects and advance reliable seismic performance assessment.
proposals.hypothesis: A single, well-calibrated numerical model, incorporating a high-fidelity constitutive law (e.g., PM4Sand or SANISAND) and explicit modeling of boundary conditions and input motion characteristics, can predict the dynamic response (e.g., accelerations, pore pressures, displacements) observed across all six LEAP-ASIA-2019 centrifuge facilities within a defined error margin.
proposals.method: The study will employ a two-dimensional, fully-coupled effective stress finite element analysis using platforms like OpenSees or PLAXIS. The constitutive model will be primarily calibrated against element test data and one reference centrifuge test. Crucially, the model will explicitly simulate each facility's specific laminar box properties, base input motion (as recorded in-flight), and model preparation effects inferred from available data. Predictions will be generated for the remaining five facilities' experiments and rigorously compared to their recorded results using quantitative metrics (e.g., error in maximum displacement, profile of excess pore pressure ratio).
proposals.contribution: This work will deliver a validated, facility-agnostic numerical framework for predicting centrifuge test outcomes, directly addressing the core challenge of inter-facility variability in LEAP. It will provide a benchmark for assessing the predictive capability of advanced constitutive models and offer practical guidance on critical modeling parameters needed to reconcile results from different experimental apparatuses.
proposals.novelty: While previous studies have back-analyzed single-facility tests, this proposal systematically inverts the problem to *predict* results across multiple, disparate facilities from a single model calibration. It moves beyond post-test simulation to a true predictive challenge, rigorously testing the universality of numerical models in geotechnical earthquake engineering.

proposals.title: Centrifuge Modeling of Liquefaction-Induced Lateral Spreading and Soil-Structure Interaction on Sloping Ground
proposals.motivation: The LEAP projects (pre-2026) have successfully benchmarked centrifuge modeling of liquefaction-induced lateral spreading in clean, uniform slopes. However, real-world geotechnical systems often involve structures founded on or within such liquefiable deposits. The interaction between a deforming, liquefied soil mass and an embedded foundation or structure remains poorly quantified, posing a significant gap in performance-based seismic design for infrastructure on or near slopes.
proposals.hypothesis: The presence of a rigid, embedded foundation will significantly alter the failure mechanism, displacement field, and excess pore pressure distribution within a liquefiable sloping ground compared to a free-field condition, and these effects can be systematically captured and scaled using centrifuge modeling.
proposals.method: The research will employ dynamic centrifuge modeling at Kyoto University, building directly on the well-characterized LEAP-UCD-2017/LEAP-ASIA-2019 Ottawa F-65 sand slope geometry and input motion protocols. A rigid model foundation (e.g., a shallow strip footing or a caisson) will be embedded within the slope. The experimental program will instrument the model with accelerometers, pore pressure transducers, and displacement markers (e.g., PIV targets) both in the free-field and adjacent to the structure. A series of tests will vary the foundation's location and embedment depth. Data analysis will focus on comparing soil-structure interaction (SSI) cases to the established free-field benchmark data to isolate the SSI effects.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide the first high-quality, openly available centrifuge dataset on liquefaction-induced lateral spreading with soil-structure interaction, directly extending the LEAP legacy. It will yield empirical insights into how structures modify lateral displacement patterns and pore pressure dissipation, offering validation data for advanced numerical constitutive models (e.g., PM4Sand, SANISAND) attempting to simulate coupled SSI in liquefiable soils.
proposals.novelty: While free-field lateral spreading is well-studied, introducing a structure into the validated LEAP slope configuration creates a new benchmark of controlled complexity. This moves beyond characterizing the hazard to quantifying the system performance, a critical step for engineering application that was not the focus of the initial LEAP phases.

proposals.title: Quantifying the Influence of Specimen Preparation Method on Cyclic and Post-Liquefaction Shear Behavior in Centrifuge Model Tests
proposals.motivation: Current centrifuge modeling of liquefaction, particularly in large collaborative projects like LEAP, predominantly relies on dry pluviation to achieve uniform, repeatable sand specimens. However, this method may not replicate the in-situ fabric and structure of naturally deposited or compacted soils, potentially biasing the observed dynamic response, pore pressure generation, and post-liquefaction deformation mechanisms. A systematic comparison with specimens prepared by wet tamping, which creates a different soil fabric, is critical for validating the general applicability of findings from pluviated models to real-world geotechnical problems.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that soil specimens prepared by wet tamping will exhibit significantly different cyclic resistance, pore pressure dissipation patterns, and post-liquefaction shear strain development during lateral spreading compared to dry-pluviated specimens of identical density, due to fundamental differences in initial soil fabric and particle arrangement.
proposals.method: The research will employ a series of centrifuge model tests at Kyoto University's facility, replicating a standardized sloping ground configuration from the LEAP projects. Identical target relative densities will be achieved using both air pluviation and moist tamping methods. Models will be instrumented with accelerometers, pore pressure transducers, and displacement markers. They will be subjected to identical base shaking motions. The key technical approach involves a direct, controlled comparison of the dynamic response (acceleration response, excess pore pressure ratio time histories) and the resulting permanent lateral displacements and shear strain localization patterns between the two preparation methods.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide a crucial dataset quantifying the specimen preparation effect on liquefaction assessment, directly informing the interpretation of past LEAP results. It will contribute improved guidelines for centrifuge model preparation when simulating specific field conditions and offer insights into how inherent soil fabric influences the triggering and consequences of liquefaction.
proposals.novelty: While preparation effects are acknowledged, this study provides a first-of-its-kind, direct experimental comparison within the rigorous, benchmarked framework of the LEAP centrifuge tests, moving beyond qualitative discussion to quantitative validation of how preparation method alters fundamental liquefaction behavior.

proposals.title: Quantifying Full-Field Soil Deformation Mechanisms in Liquefaction-Induced Lateral Spreading Using Centrifuge Modeling and High-Speed Digital Image Correlation
proposals.motivation: Understanding the full-field, transient deformation of soil during seismic liquefaction and lateral spreading is critical for assessing geotechnical infrastructure risk. Current centrifuge test validation for numerical models (e.g., LEAP projects) relies heavily on sparse discrete sensor data, which cannot capture the complete, heterogeneous displacement field, especially within the soil mass and at soil-structure interfaces. A method to obtain dense, high-resolution deformation data is needed to rigorously validate advanced constitutive models and improve predictive capabilities.
proposals.hypothesis: The application of high-speed Digital Image Correlation (DIC) to centrifuge models will reveal previously unmeasured, complex soil deformation mechanisms—such as shear band formation, soil flow around embedded elements, and the evolution of displacement gradients—during liquefaction-induced lateral spreading, providing a ground truth dataset that discrete sensors cannot capture.
proposals.method: We will conduct a series of dynamic centrifuge tests on sloping saturated sand models, replicating LEAP-ASIA-2019 prototype conditions. A high-speed camera mounted on the centrifuge will capture images through a transparent sidewall. A stochastic speckle pattern will be applied to the soil model. Pre-2026 DIC algorithms (e.g., normalized cross-correlation or iterative least-squares matching) will be used to compute full-field displacement vectors (u, w) and derived strain fields. The procedure will be synchronized with accelerometer and pore pressure transducer data. Specific validation will involve comparing DIC-derived displacements at sensor locations with physical measurements (e.g., LVDTs).
proposals.contribution: This work will produce the first high-resolution, full-field deformation dataset for a standardized LEAP centrifuge model, serving as a benchmark for validating high-fidelity numerical simulations (e.g., MPM, DEM). It will advance the fundamental understanding of soil flow mechanisms during lateral spreading. The developed DIC protocol will provide a new standard for quantitative analysis in physical modeling of geotechnical seismic problems.
proposals.novelty: While DIC has been used in 1-g geotechnical testing, its application to dynamic centrifuge modeling, particularly for liquefaction problems, remains limited and challenging due to high-g and seismic excitation. This proposal integrates established DIC methodology directly into the validated, community-standard LEAP experimental framework to extract unprecedented deformation details from a well-characterized benchmark test.

proposals.title: A Coupled SPH-FEM Framework for Simulating Post-Liquefaction Soil Flow and Its Validation Against Centrifuge Model Tests
proposals.motivation: Predicting large, catastrophic deformations and flow failures in liquefied soils remains a critical challenge in geotechnical earthquake engineering. Conventional finite element methods (FEM) struggle with extreme mesh distortion during such events. Mesh-free methods from fluid dynamics, like Smoothed Particle Hydrodynamics (SPH), offer a promising alternative for modeling soil as a fluid-like continuum undergoing severe deformation, but their rigorous application and validation for geotechnical problems are limited.
proposals.hypothesis: A coupled SPH-FEM computational framework, where SPH models the liquefied soil flow zone and FEM models the surrounding stable soil and structures, can accurately capture the initiation and runout of liquefaction-induced lateral spreading, validated against high-quality centrifuge model test data.
proposals.method: The research will develop a two-phase SPH formulation for saturated soil, incorporating a critical state soil constitutive model (e.g., a modified Cam-clay type model) to represent soil behavior from solid to fluidized states. The SPH domain will be dynamically coupled to a traditional FEM domain using a master-slave node/particle contact algorithm. The framework will be implemented and tested using open-source codes (e.g., LAMMPS for SPH, OpenSees for FEM). The primary validation will be against T. Tobita's existing centrifuge test data from LEAP projects on sloping ground and lateral spreading.
proposals.contribution: This work will deliver a validated, open numerical tool for simulating post-liquefaction flow failure, directly addressing a key gap in performance-based earthquake engineering. It will provide new insights into the transition from seismic shaking to soil flow and offer a practical analysis method for assessing the stability of earth structures and foundations under extreme seismic loading.
proposals.novelty: While SPH has been explored for landslides, its rigorous coupling with FEM and application specifically to validated, centrifuge-modeled seismically liquefied soil flow, using a robust critical state soil model within the SPH framework, represents a significant advancement beyond current state-of-the-art.

proposals.title: Quantifying the 3D Boundary Effect on Liquefaction-Induced Deformation: A Centrifuge and Numerical Modeling Study of Sloping Ground
proposals.motivation: Geotechnical centrifuge modeling and numerical analysis of seismic soil-structure interaction often rely on 2D plane-strain assumptions for practicality. However, real-world problems are inherently three-dimensional, and the constraints of a rigid centrifuge container wall introduce complex boundary effects that are not captured in 2D analyses. A systematic comparison is needed to quantify how these 3D confinement conditions alter key engineering responses, such as excess pore pressure generation, lateral displacement, and shear strain localization, compared to idealized 2D plane-strain conditions.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that 3D boundary conditions in a centrifuge model, characterized by side-wall friction and partial confinement, will significantly suppress lateral spreading displacements and alter the shear band formation pattern compared to a true 2D plane-strain condition, leading to non-conservative estimates if not properly accounted for in design.
proposals.method: The research will employ a combined physical and numerical modeling approach. First, a new series of 3D dynamic centrifuge tests on saturated sloping sand deposits will be conducted at Kyoto University, instrumented with pore pressure transducers, accelerometers, and displacement markers. These will be directly compared to existing 2D plane-strain centrifuge data (e.g., from LEAP projects). Second, high-fidelity 3D finite element simulations will be performed using an advanced constitutive model (e.g., PM4Sand or a cyclic elastoplastic model) in platforms like OpenSees or FLAC3D. The numerical models will explicitly simulate the container walls and the model's three-dimensional geometry to decouple the material response from the boundary effects.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide a validated, quantitative framework for correcting 2D analysis results to account for 3D boundary effects in centrifuge testing. It will deliver specific guidance on the degree of conservatism (or lack thereof) inherent in interpreting 2D centrifuge data for lateral spreading problems. The results will directly improve the reliability of physical and numerical models used for seismic performance assessment of geotechnical systems.
proposals.novelty: While 3D centrifuge tests exist, a controlled, direct comparison isolating the effect of dimensionality (3D with boundaries vs. idealized 2D) for liquefaction-induced deformation, backed by parallel high-fidelity 3D numerical analysis, is novel. This moves beyond qualitative recognition of the issue to a quantitative, mechanics-based correction methodology.

proposals.title: Quantifying Boundary Effects in Centrifuge Modeling of Liquefaction-Induced Deformations: Validation of Numerical Models Using Flexible-Wall Container Experiments
proposals.motivation: Centrifuge modeling is a critical tool for validating numerical simulations of soil liquefaction and lateral spreading. However, the rigid boundaries of conventional model containers impose non-physical constraints on soil deformation, potentially distorting the validation process. A systematic assessment of these boundary effects is needed to improve confidence in model predictions for critical infrastructure.
proposals.hypothesis: Numerical models calibrated and validated against centrifuge tests conducted in flexible-wall containers will demonstrate superior predictive accuracy for liquefaction-induced ground deformations compared to those validated using rigid-wall container data, by more faithfully replicating field-scale boundary conditions.
proposals.method: The research will conduct a series of dynamic centrifuge tests on saturated, sloping sand models at Kyoto University's geotechnical centrifuge facility, employing a flexible-wall (e.g., laminar or equivalent shear beam) container. Key instrumentation will include accelerometers, pore pressure transducers, and displacement sensors. The experimental results will serve as benchmark data to validate advanced constitutive models (e.g., PM4Sand, SANISAND) implemented in finite element software (e.g., OpenSees, FLAC). The validation will involve a direct, quantitative comparison of predicted and measured accelerations, excess pore pressures, and permanent lateral displacements.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide a high-quality, publicly available experimental dataset from flexible-boundary tests for the validation community. It will deliver specific guidance on the magnitude and nature of boundary effects in centrifuge validation exercises, thereby improving the reliability of numerical models used in seismic hazard assessment and the design of mitigation measures.
proposals.novelty: While flexible containers are used in research, their systematic application as a dedicated validation benchmark for numerical models, specifically targeting the quantification of rigid-boundary error in liquefaction deformation predictions, represents a focused advancement beyond the current state of practice in the LEAP projects.

proposals.title: Full-Scale Validation of Centrifuge-Based Predictions for Liquefaction-Induced Deformation of Slopes and Embankments
proposals.motivation: Centrifuge modeling is a cornerstone of geotechnical earthquake engineering, providing critical data for validating numerical models and informing design. However, the ultimate test of these scaled models and the constitutive models they calibrate is their ability to predict the performance of real, full-scale geostructures. A rigorous, instrumented comparison between model predictions and full-scale field behavior remains a significant gap, limiting confidence in extrapolating laboratory results to practice.
proposals.hypothesis: That the deformation mechanisms and magnitudes predicted for liquefaction-induced lateral spreading using state-of-the-art centrifuge models and associated numerical simulations will show quantifiable and explainable agreement with the recorded performance of instrumented, full-scale embankments that have undergone liquefaction.
proposals.method: This research will conduct a forensic analysis of well-documented, full-scale case histories (e.g., embankment damage from the 2011 Christchurch or 2018 Hokkaido earthquakes) where high-quality ground motion records and post-event deformation surveys exist. Advanced, validated constitutive models (e.g., PM4Sand, SANISAND), calibrated using data from T. Tobita's previous LEAP centrifuge tests, will be used in 2D/3D finite element analyses (e.g., OpenSees, FLAC) to simulate the field case. The centrifuge test results themselves will be treated as a physical simulation of the prototype, with predictions scaled to prototype dimensions using established generalized scaling laws. The simulated and scaled centrifuge results will be quantitatively compared against the field-recorded accelerations, pore pressures, and permanent displacements.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide a rare, direct benchmark for the predictive capability of centrifuge modeling and advanced constitutive models at the field scale. It will identify the primary sources of discrepancy between model and reality, guiding future improvements in testing protocols and numerical modeling. The outcome will strengthen the theoretical and practical foundation for using physical modeling in performance-based earthquake engineering for liquefiable soils.
proposals.novelty: While centrifuge-to-numerical model validation is common, this proposal systematically links the entire centrifuge modeling chain—from physical model to constitutive model calibration to numerical prediction—to a specific, instrumented field failure, a comprehensive validation exercise rarely undertaken. It leverages the unique, high-quality benchmark data from the LEAP projects to interrogate the scalability of the entire methodology.

proposals.title: Validation of a Generalized Scaling Law for Liquefaction-Induced Deformation in Non-Sandy Soils: Centrifuge Experiments on Silt and Low-Plasticity Clay
proposals.motivation: The LEAP (Liquefaction Experiments and Analysis Projects) initiatives have successfully developed and validated a generalized scaling law and testing protocols for clean sands, a critical step for reliable physical modeling. However, many natural and engineered soil deposits, such as silts and clays with low plasticity, are also susceptible to cyclic softening and significant deformation during earthquakes. The applicability of the established scaling laws and validation frameworks to these fine-grained soils remains unverified, creating a gap in our ability to model and predict a wider range of geohazards.
proposals.hypothesis: The generalized scaling law and standardized centrifuge testing protocol validated for sands can be effectively applied to predict the seismic response and deformation of saturated silts and low-plasticity clays, provided soil-specific index properties and permeability effects are appropriately accounted for in the scaling relationships.
proposals.method: This research will employ centrifuge modeling at Kyoto University's facility, following the standardized LEAP testing protocol for sloping ground. We will prepare identical model geometries but with three soil types: a reference clean sand (Toyoura sand), a non-plastic silt (Silica flour or crushed silica), and a low-plasticity clay (e.g., Kaolin clay). Each model will be saturated and subjected to a suite of scaled earthquake motions of varying intensity. Key measurements will include pore water pressure generation, acceleration response, and lateral displacement. The experimental results for the fine-grained soils will be compared against predictions made by applying the existing generalized scaling law, with adjustments for permeability and consolidation time based on established centrifuge scaling principles.
proposals.contribution: This work will directly test the boundaries of a key validation framework in physical modeling, providing crucial empirical evidence on its applicability to non-sandy soils. It will generate a high-quality, publicly available benchmark dataset for silts and clays, enabling the validation of numerical constitutive models for these materials. The findings will inform future revisions to scaling laws and improve risk assessment for liquefaction and lateral spreading in diverse soil profiles.
proposals.novelty: While LEAP has focused on sand validation, this proposal systematically extends the rigorous validation exercise to fine-grained soils, which is a logical but experimentally unexplored next step. The explicit test of a scaling law developed for sands on cohesive and intermediate soils addresses a fundamental question in geotechnical physical modeling.

proposals.title: Quantifying the Influence of Slope Geometry on Liquefaction-Induced Deformation: A Centrifuge Model Test Study
proposals.motivation: Liquefaction-induced lateral spreading on slopes is a major cause of damage during earthquakes, but current predictive models often rely on simplified geometry assumptions. The LEAP projects have established benchmark data for specific slopes, yet a systematic investigation of how varying slope angle and height independently and interactively affect the onset, extent, and pattern of deformation is lacking. This gap limits the accuracy of risk assessments for natural slopes, embankments, and excavated ground, which are common in civil and geotechnical engineering projects.
proposals.hypothesis: The post-liquefaction deformation mechanism and magnitude are not linearly related to slope angle or height; instead, a critical geometry threshold exists beyond which failure transitions from localized soil movement to a coherent sliding block, significantly amplifying displacements.
proposals.method: The research will employ a series of dynamic centrifuge model tests at Kyoto University's facility, building on the proven LEAP testing protocols. A saturated, uniform fine silica sand will be used to construct slopes with systematically varied geometries (e.g., angles of 2, 5, and 10 degrees; heights of 5m and 10m in prototype scale). Each model will be instrumented with pore pressure transducers and accelerometers, and subjected to identical base shaking motions. The key technical approach involves using Particle Image Velocimetry (PIV) analysis on high-speed camera footage to precisely quantify the deformation fields and displacement vectors for each geometry.
proposals.contribution: This work will produce a unique, high-quality dataset linking specific slope geometries to measurable deformation outcomes, directly validating and improving numerical models. It will contribute empirical scaling relationships for deformation magnitude as a function of slope angle and height, providing engineers with more reliable inputs for performance-based design and hazard mapping.
proposals.novelty: While previous studies like LEAP have focused on validating models for a single slope, this proposal systematically decouples and tests the fundamental geometric variables (angle and height) to isolate their individual and combined effects on liquefaction response, moving from validation to fundamental parametric investigation.

proposals.title: Centrifuge Modeling of Liquefaction-Induced Lateral Spreading and Its Interaction with Pile Foundations on Sloping Ground
proposals.motivation: Liquefaction-induced lateral spreading during earthquakes poses a severe threat to structures founded on or within slopes, such as bridge piers, retaining walls, and building foundations. While past centrifuge studies (e.g., LEAP projects) have characterized the free-field soil response, the complex interaction between the spreading soil and embedded structural elements remains less quantified, leading to uncertainties in seismic design. This research directly addresses this gap by integrating model structures into well-characterized slope models.
proposals.hypothesis: The presence of pile foundations will significantly alter the kinematic field and excess pore pressure generation/dissipation in a liquefiable sloping ground compared to the free-field condition, and the resulting soil-structure interaction forces will be highly dependent on the relative stiffness and embedment depth of the piles.
proposals.method: We will conduct a series of dynamic centrifuge tests at Kyoto University, building directly on the LEAP-UCD-2017 and LEAP-ASIA-2019 sloping ground models. Instrumented model pile groups or shallow foundations will be installed in the prototype slope. The tests will employ the same saturated silica sand and input motions used in prior LEAP studies to ensure baseline comparability. Key measurements will include pile bending moments via strain gauges, soil accelerations, pore pressures, and lateral displacements using PIV (Particle Image Velocimetry) to capture the full soil-structure interaction mechanism.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide a unique, high-quality experimental dataset of soil-structure interaction in liquefying slopes, validating and improving numerical modeling techniques. It will yield practical insights for calibrating p-y spring models for liquefied soil and inform performance-based design guidelines for foundations vulnerable to lateral spreading.
proposals.novelty: This study uniquely combines the rigorous, validated experimental framework of the international LEAP projects with the direct investigation of structural elements, moving beyond free-field characterization to isolate the two-way interaction mechanics in a controlled, benchmarked setting.

proposals.title: Validation of Centrifuge Scaling Laws for Liquefaction-Induced Deformation through a Full-Scale, Instrumented, Submerged Sand Slope Field Test
proposals.motivation: Centrifuge modeling is a cornerstone of geotechnical earthquake engineering, particularly for studying soil liquefaction. However, the validation of its scaling laws and the extrapolation of results to prototype scale rely on indirect comparisons and numerical back-analyses. A direct, controlled, full-scale physical validation is critically lacking, creating uncertainty in applying centrifuge findings to real-world civil infrastructure design.
proposals.hypothesis: The generalized scaling laws for dynamic centrifuge modeling, particularly for pore pressure generation, dissipation, and lateral displacement in submerged slopes, will be validated when a meticulously constructed full-scale slope, subjected to controlled shaking, exhibits response histories that match the time-scaled responses observed in equivalent centrifuge models.
proposals.method: We will construct a large, fully submerged, uniform sand slope (approx. 10m high, 30m long) at a suitable field site. The slope will be instrumented with an array of pore pressure transducers, accelerometers, and displacement sensors (LVDTs, inclinometers) replicating the layout used in the LEAP-ASIA-2019 centrifuge tests. A large-scale mobile shaker (e.g., a vibroseis truck) will provide controlled, scaled seismic input motions. Data acquisition will capture the full, prototype-scale time history of the dynamic response. The results will be directly compared to the time-scaled data from the well-documented Kyoto University centrifuge models, focusing on the evolution of excess pore pressure and permanent lateral displacement.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide the first high-quality, fully instrumented dataset of liquefaction-induced deformation in a controlled, full-scale geotechnical structure. It will serve as a definitive benchmark for validating centrifuge scaling laws and high-fidelity numerical models. The findings will directly improve confidence in using centrifuge testing to assess the seismic performance of dams, embankments, and waterfront structures.
proposals.novelty: While centrifuge tests and numerical simulations are abundant, a purpose-built, fully instrumented, large-scale physical model designed explicitly for the validation of centrifuge scaling laws for liquefaction is unprecedented. This bridges the critical gap between physical scale modeling and real-world prototype behavior.

proposals.title: Calibration of Constitutive Models for Liquefiable Sands: Linking Element-Level Cyclic Simple Shear Response to Centrifuge Model Performance
proposals.motivation: Centrifuge modeling, as used in projects like LEAP, is a vital tool for validating numerical simulations of liquefaction-induced deformations. However, discrepancies between different centrifuge facilities and model tests persist, often attributed to complex boundary conditions and system-level interactions. Isolating the fundamental cyclic stress-strain response of the soil material itself is crucial for improving and calibrating the constitutive models used in these simulations.
proposals.hypothesis: The cyclic simple shear response of a well-characterized sand, tested under stress paths representative of field conditions, provides a unique and boundary-effect-free benchmark. This elemental response data can be used to calibrate constitutive model parameters that will reduce scatter in the prediction of system-level centrifuge model performance.
proposals.method: We will conduct a comprehensive series of stress-controlled cyclic simple shear (CSS) tests on Ottawa F-65 sand, identical to that used in LEAP experiments. Tests will replicate the initial stress states and cyclic stress ratios (CSR) experienced at critical depths within the LEAP centrifuge models. The resulting pore pressure generation and shear strain accumulation data will serve as the calibration target. We will then calibrate two widely used pre-2026 constitutive models (e.g., a Pastor-Zienkiewicz Mark III model and a PM4Sand model) against this CSS dataset. The calibrated parameters will be implemented in finite element simulations of the LEAP-UCD-2017 centrifuge test for validation.
proposals.contribution: This work will produce a high-quality, publicly available dataset of elemental sand response under controlled cyclic loading, directly linked to a major international validation exercise (LEAP). It will demonstrate a rigorous methodology for constitutive model calibration, moving from element to system level. The outcome will be a clearer understanding of how much predictive uncertainty stems from material model parameters versus other modeling assumptions.
proposals.novelty: While CSS tests and model calibration are common, this proposal systematically connects a specific, boundary-effect-free element test on a benchmark sand to the performance of a specific, well-documented centrifuge model, creating a closed loop for validation. It uses the LEAP framework not for model comparison, but for targeted calibration based on isolated material behavior.

proposals.title: Three-Dimensional Centrifuge Modeling of Liquefaction-Induced Lateral Spreading in Sloping Ground: Quantifying Out-of-Plane Displacements and Shear Strain Localization
proposals.motivation: Current understanding of seismic soil liquefaction and lateral spreading relies heavily on 2D plane-strain centrifuge tests and analyses. Real geotechnical structures, such as embankments, levees, and natural slopes, are inherently three-dimensional, with complex boundary conditions and soil heterogeneity that can induce significant out-of-plane deformations. Pre-2026, the validation of numerical models against 3D physical data is limited, creating a critical gap in our ability to accurately assess the seismic performance and failure mechanisms of these critical earth systems.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that 3D shaking in a centrifuge will generate measurably different patterns of liquefaction-induced displacement, pore pressure dissipation, and shear band formation compared to equivalent 2D shaking tests, due to the release of out-of-plane constraints and the potential for complex wave scattering and soil-structure interaction.
proposals.method: We will utilize the 3D shaking table on the geotechnical centrifuge at Kyoto University. A uniform, saturated sloping sand deposit will be constructed in a rigid container, instrumented with pore pressure transducers, accelerometers, and displacement markers. A series of tests will be conducted, systematically varying the direction and components of the input seismic motion (e.g., unidirectional vs. multi-directional shaking). The dynamic response will be captured using high-speed cameras and PIV (Particle Image Velocimetry) analysis to extract full-field 3D displacement vectors. The results will be directly compared to legacy 2D data from the LEAP-UCD-2017 and LEAP-ASIA-2019 projects.
proposals.contribution: This work will produce a first-of-its-kind, high-quality 3D centrifuge dataset for a benchmark liquefaction problem, serving as a crucial validation target for advanced 3D numerical constitutive models. It will quantify the practical significance of out-of-plane effects on lateral displacement magnitudes and patterns, informing the conservatism or limitations of current 2D-based design practices for earth structures.
proposals.novelty: While 3D centrifuge shakers exist, their application to a canonical, well-studied problem like the LEAP liquefaction slope tests to isolate and quantify purely three-dimensional effects is novel. This moves beyond qualitative observation to provide quantitative metrics on how 3D freedom alters fundamental liquefaction mechanics.

proposals.title: Inter-facility Benchmarking of Transient Seepage in Centrifuge Models: Quantifying Scaling Distortions in Coarse-Grained Rubble
proposals.motivation: Recent collaborative projects like LEAP have revealed significant inter-facility divergence in centrifuge test results for geotechnical problems involving seepage and liquefaction. For problems like the performance of rubble-mound structures or tailings dams under transient hydraulic loading, the scaling of seepage flow is critical, yet the reproducibility of such tests across different centrifuges remains unverified. This undermines confidence in centrifuge modeling as a tool for validating numerical codes and assessing critical infrastructure.
proposals.hypothesis: A primary source of inter-facility result divergence is facility-specific distortion in the scaling of transient seepage through coarse-grained materials, arising from differences in model construction, acceleration history, pore fluid properties, and sensor response.
proposals.method: A standardized, instrumented benchmark experiment for one-dimensional transient seepage through a uniform coarse sand (e.g., Toyoura sand) will be designed. Identical model specifications, including geometry, relative density, and initial saturation, will be distributed to at least three centrifuge facilities (e.g., Kyoto University, UC Davis, RPI). Each facility will execute the test, monitoring pore pressure dissipation during a controlled acceleration ramp and hold phase. Data will be analyzed by comparing the non-dimensional time for 50% pore pressure dissipation against the theoretical scaling law (t_model = t_prototype / N^2), isolating deviations. The analysis will correlate distortions with specific facility parameters like fluid viscosity, g-ramp rate, and porous stone/sensor interface design.
proposals.contribution: This work will produce the first community dataset explicitly quantifying facility-specific seepage scaling errors. It will yield a practical framework, potentially in the form of facility-specific correction factors or bounded error envelopes, to improve the reproducibility of centrifuge tests for seepage-driven problems. This directly supports more reliable physical modeling for the validation of constitutive models and numerical codes in geotechnical engineering.
proposals.novelty: While LEAP focused on seismic liquefaction, this proposal uniquely targets the scaling of transient seepage—a pervasive but often overlooked distortion mechanism. It moves beyond documenting result differences to actively diagnosing their hydraulic root cause through a controlled, multi-facility benchmark, a novel approach in centrifuge methodology.

proposals.title: A Dimensionally-Aware Material Point Method for the Scale-Consistent Prediction of Breakwater Collapse Under Seismic Loading
proposals.motivation: The design of coastal breakwaters against seismic liquefaction and collapse relies heavily on centrifuge modeling, but extrapolating results to prototype scale remains challenging. Current high-fidelity numerical methods like the Material Point Method (MPM) can simulate large deformations but lack an explicit, physics-grounded framework for ensuring simulations adhere to the correct scaling laws, limiting their predictive reliability for real-world structures.
proposals.hypothesis: By embedding scaling laws derived from dimensional analysis directly into an MPM framework, the resulting 'dimensionally-aware' model can be rigorously calibrated at the centrifuge scale and then used to accurately predict prototype-scale collapse mechanisms and performance.
proposals.method: First, Buckingham Pi theorem will be applied to identify the non-dimensional Pi groups governing the seismic response and liquefaction-induced failure of saturated soil breakwaters. These groups will then be explicitly enforced within a 3D, two-phase (soil-water) MPM code. This involves mapping the Pi groups to constrain the input parameters (e.g., permeability, constitutive model coefficients) and boundary conditions for simulations at different scales. The method will be validated against T. Tobita's existing centrifuge test data from the LEAP projects and new centrifuge experiments on breakwater models.
proposals.contribution: This work will produce a novel computational geomechanics framework that intrinsically bridges scale gaps, providing a more reliable tool for performance-based design of geostructures. It will deliver validated scaling relationships for breakwater collapse and demonstrate a methodology for fusing dimensional analysis with advanced numerical simulation to enhance predictive capability.
proposals.novelty: The explicit, programmatic fusion of dimensional analysis scaling laws with the MPM's large-deformation formulation to create a scale-aware simulation tool is a novel approach not present in pre-2026 geotechnical numerical modeling.

proposals.title: Full-Scale Prototype Validation of Centrifuge-Derived Seepage and Liquefaction Mitigation Strategies for Breakwater Stability
proposals.motivation: Coastal breakwaters are critical infrastructure vulnerable to seismic liquefaction and internal seepage erosion, as evidenced by past failures. While centrifuge modeling, such as the LEAP projects, has advanced our understanding of these phenomena, a critical gap exists in validating these scaled findings at full prototype scale. Direct validation is essential for confidently translating laboratory-derived design guidelines into engineering practice for resilient maritime structures.
proposals.hypothesis: The mechanisms of seepage-induced internal erosion and liquefaction-induced deformation in a breakwater core, as characterized in centrifuge models, will be accurately replicated and measurable in a fully instrumented, full-scale prototype under controlled hydraulic and seismic loading.
proposals.method: We will construct a full-scale, instrumented section of a caisson-type breakwater with a gravel-mound core at a suitable coastal test site. The core will integrate a designed seepage control system (e.g., graded filters). The structure will be instrumented with pore pressure transducers, accelerometers, displacement sensors, and seepage flow meters. It will first be subjected to controlled, staged hydraulic loading via a pumping system to induce internal seepage. Subsequently, it will be dynamically tested using a large-scale mobile shaker or explosive charges to simulate seismic excitation, monitoring its liquefaction response and stability.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide the first comprehensive dataset directly comparing centrifuge and full-scale prototype behavior for a complex geotechnical structure under combined seepage and seismic loads. It will deliver a crucial validation step for centrifuge scaling laws and numerical models, leading to more reliable performance-based design methods for breakwaters and similar earth structures.
proposals.novelty: This proposal moves beyond state-of-the-art centrifuge validation exercises (e.g., LEAP) by conducting controlled destructive testing on a fully instrumented, engineered full-scale prototype, a step rarely undertaken due to cost and complexity, to provide definitive evidence for scaling laws.

proposals.title: A Physics-Informed Neural Network Framework for Correcting 1-g Shaking Table Data to Prototype-Scale Predictions in Liquefiable Slopes
proposals.motivation: 1-g shaking table tests are rapid and cost-effective for parametric studies of soil-structure systems but suffer from significant scaling distortions, particularly for liquefaction phenomena where stress-dependent soil behavior is critical. High-fidelity centrifuge modeling and numerical simulations are accurate but prohibitively expensive for large-scale parameter exploration. A hybrid method that corrects 1-g data to prototype scale would dramatically accelerate research into seismic performance of geotechnical systems.
proposals.hypothesis: A deep learning model, trained on paired datasets from high-fidelity centrifuge tests and DEM/FEM simulations, can learn a generalized distortion-correction function that maps the response of a 1-g shaking table model to its equivalent prototype-scale behavior.
proposals.method: We will generate a foundational dataset from existing LEAP centrifuge experiments (e.g., LEAP-ASIA-2019) and complementary high-resolution DEM/FEM simulations of liquefiable sloping ground. A convolutional neural network (CNN) or a U-Net architecture will be trained on this dataset to map time-series and spatial output (e.g., pore pressure, displacement) from a 1-g test to the target prototype-scale response. The loss function will incorporate physics-based constraints (e.g., conservation laws) to ensure physical plausibility. The trained model will then be validated on new, unseen 1-g test data from a bespoke experimental campaign.
proposals.contribution: This work will produce a novel, validated computational tool that enables rapid, physics-informed parameter studies using 1-g tables, effectively bridging the gap between low-cost screening and high-fidelity analysis. It will provide a new methodology for the geotechnical earthquake engineering community to efficiently explore design spaces and failure mechanisms for liquefaction-prone sites.
proposals.novelty: While ML has been used for system identification, applying a physics-informed CNN as a learned scaling law to correct fundamental 1-g model distortions for geotechnical applications is a novel integration of experimental mechanics, scaling laws, and machine learning.

proposals.title: Quantifying Boundary Effects in 1-g Seismic Soil-Structure Interaction: A High-Throughput Experimental Framework Using Programmable Flexible Containers
proposals.motivation: In 1-g shaking table tests, boundary conditions of the soil container significantly influence the measured dynamic response, including shear stress distribution and pore pressure generation, complicating the extrapolation of results to field conditions. While rigid and laminar boxes are standard, their fixed properties limit systematic study of boundary effects, creating uncertainty in validating numerical models. A method to efficiently decouple boundary influence from intrinsic soil response is needed to improve the reliability of small-scale physical modeling for geotechnical earthquake engineering.
proposals.hypothesis: The dynamic response of a soil model and embedded structure in 1-g shaking tests is a predictable function of the programmable stiffness and damping of the container boundaries, allowing for the isolation and quantification of boundary effects from the true soil-structure interaction mechanism.
proposals.method: We will design and fabricate a suite of low-cost, instrumented flexible containers where sidewall stiffness is controlled via adjustable spring-damper arrays or programmable pneumatic actuators. A series of high-throughput tests will be conducted on saturated silica sand, replicating a sloping ground or a simple foundation model, using a uniaxial shaking table. Test parameters will systematically vary boundary stiffness/damping and input motion intensity. Responses, including accelerations, displacements, and pore pressures, will be compared to results from a calibrated numerical model (e.g., OpenSees or FLIP) with explicitly simulated flexible boundaries to develop correction factors.
proposals.contribution: This work will produce a unique, open-access database of high-quality experimental data linking boundary properties to system response, specifically for liquefaction and lateral spreading scenarios. It will provide practical guidance for selecting and justifying boundary conditions in 1-g testing and deliver validated numerical modeling techniques to account for boundary effects, enhancing the fidelity of physical model studies.
proposals.novelty: The novelty lies in the high-throughput, parametric approach using actively programmable container boundaries, moving beyond fixed laminar or rigid walls, to systematically isolate boundary effects—a factor often acknowledged but rarely quantified in a controlled, repeatable manner for 1-g shaking tests.

proposals.title: A Micromechanical Framework for Predicting the Cyclic Strength and Hydraulic Conductivity of Silty Sands and Layered Soils
proposals.motivation: Geotechnical engineering practice often relies on models calibrated for clean sands, yet natural and excavated soils frequently contain fines or exhibit layering, which drastically alters their mechanical and hydraulic response, particularly under seismic loading leading to liquefaction. Current empirical corrections for fines content are often inconsistent and lack a fundamental physical basis, creating uncertainty in the assessment of critical infrastructure like dams and slopes. This research addresses this gap by establishing a physics-based, microstructural link between soil composition and macro-scale engineering properties.
proposals.hypothesis: The macro-scale cyclic strength, stiffness, and hydraulic conductivity of soils with fines or thin layers can be predicted by applying composite material micromechanics, where the soil is treated as a multi-phase material, and its effective properties are derived from the volume fraction, properties, and spatial arrangement of its constituents (e.g., sand grains, silt matrix, clay layers).
proposals.method: The research will combine physical experiments, discrete element method (DEM) simulations, and analytical homogenization. First, a suite of centrifuge model tests on layered and silty sand profiles will be conducted to capture macro-scale seismic response and pore pressure dissipation. Concurrently, 3D DEM simulations will generate virtual soil specimens to quantify micro-scale force chains and fabric evolution. These datasets will calibrate and validate a Representative Volume Element (RVE) approach, implementing the Mori-Tanaka and self-consistent schemes to homogenize the behavior of the distinct phases. The final output will be a constitutive model framework where parameters are explicitly linked to measurable microstructural descriptors.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide a novel, mechanics-based constitutive framework for silty and layered soils, moving beyond purely empirical correlations. It will deliver predictive models for strength and permeability that directly incorporate microstructure, improving the accuracy of liquefaction hazard evaluation for complex soil profiles. The results will advance the fundamental understanding of seismic soil-structure interaction for dams and embankments founded on or comprising such materials.
proposals.novelty: While composite mechanics is well-established in materials science, its systematic application to predict the coupled hydro-mechanical behavior of soils, particularly for seismic problems, is a significant and underexplored crossover. The explicit integration of DEM-derived fabric metrics with analytical homogenization to create a practical engineering model represents a novel synthesis of methods in geomechanics.

proposals.title: Centrifuge Modeling of Seismic Response and Liquefaction-Induced Deformation in Layered and Silty Sand Deposits
proposals.motivation: Most centrifuge validation studies for liquefaction, such as the LEAP projects, have focused on uniform, clean sands. However, natural soil deposits often contain fines or are layered, which significantly alters their permeability, cyclic strength, and post-liquefaction deformation mechanisms. A systematic experimental investigation of these realistic soil conditions is critical for advancing predictive models and improving the seismic safety of geotechnical structures.
proposals.hypothesis: The presence of fines and soil layering will fundamentally alter the pore pressure dissipation pathways and shear strain localization during seismic shaking, leading to lateral spreading displacements and ground failure patterns that are not captured by models calibrated for clean sand.
proposals.method: We will conduct a series of dynamic centrifuge tests at Kyoto University, building on the LEAP experimental protocols. We will prepare model slopes and level ground with controlled layered profiles (e.g., a silt layer sandwiched between sand layers) and homogeneous silty sands with varying fines content (e.g., 10-30%). The models will be instrumented with accelerometers, pore pressure transducers, and displacement markers. Seismic excitation will be applied using the in-flight shaker, and the response will be compared directly to benchmark LEAP clean sand data. The Generalized Scaling Law will be used to interpret results across different centrifugal accelerations.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide a high-quality, publicly available experimental dataset on complex soil stratigraphies, filling a major gap in validation benchmarks. It will improve the physical understanding of how fines content and layering affect liquefaction triggering and consequent deformations, directly informing the development and calibration of next-generation constitutive models and numerical simulation tools.
proposals.novelty: While layered systems have been studied statically, their dynamic response under well-controlled, validated centrifuge conditions matching the LEAP framework is novel. This study explicitly bridges the gap between simplified validation experiments and the complexity of in-situ stratigraphy.

proposals.title: A Controlled Factorial Experiment to Isolate Causal Factors in Inter-Facility Variance for Centrifuge Modeling of Liquefiable Slopes
proposals.motivation: Large collaborative projects like LEAP have revealed significant inter-facility variance in centrifuge test results for liquefaction problems, even with shared specifications. This variance undermines confidence in the absolute predictive capability of physical modeling and complicates the validation of numerical tools. A systematic, causal investigation is needed to move beyond retrospective correlation and guide future standardization efforts in geotechnical earthquake engineering.
proposals.hypothesis: The primary contributors to inter-facility variance in centrifuge modeling of liquefaction-induced deformation are not random but can be causally attributed to specific, identifiable factors related to model preparation, boundary conditions, and data acquisition. The relative contribution of these factors can be quantified.
proposals.method: A standardized, densely instrumented model of a sloping liquefiable sand layer will be designed. This test package will be replicated at multiple centrifuge facilities (e.g., Kyoto University, others in the LEAP network). Using a factorial experimental design, key factors (e.g., sand pluviation method/energy, pore fluid viscosity, shaking protocol refinement, sensor type/calibration) will be deliberately and systematically varied in a controlled manner at each site according to the design matrix. All facilities will follow a common core protocol. The resulting data on accelerations, pore pressures, and displacements will be analyzed using Analysis of Variance (ANOVA) to partition the total observed variance and attribute it to the manipulated factors.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide the first causally grounded quantification of error sources in geotechnical centrifuge testing, directly informing best practices for model preparation and data collection. It will yield a robust empirical framework for interpreting past collaborative test data and designing future validation exercises, ultimately increasing the reliability of physical models for performance-based earthquake engineering.
proposals.novelty: Unlike previous comparative studies that analyzed existing data, this is a proactively designed physical experiment where potential error sources are deliberately introduced as controlled variables. This shifts the methodology from passive observation to active causal inference, a novel approach in geotechnical physical modeling.

proposals.title: Diagnosing Facility-Specific Bias in Centrifuge Modeling of Liquefaction: A Multi-Metric Response Signature Framework
proposals.motivation: The LEAP (Liquefaction Experiments and Analysis Projects) initiatives, such as LEAP-UCD-2017 and LEAP-ASIA-2019, have revealed significant inter-facility discrepancies in centrifuge test results for identical model specifications. These discrepancies, often attributed to subtle differences in model preparation, boundary conditions, and instrumentation, undermine the reliability of physical modeling for validating numerical tools and understanding soil liquefaction. A systematic method is needed to move beyond simple displacement or pore pressure comparisons and pinpoint the root causes of these biases to improve experimental protocols across the geotechnical community.
proposals.hypothesis: Facility-specific biases in centrifuge modeling manifest as distinct, correlated patterns across a suite of response metrics (e.g., acceleration spectra, shear strain localization, stress paths, and system energy). Analyzing these multi-metric signatures can differentiate between inherent soil variability and systematic experimental error, isolating the specific aspects of model construction or testing that cause the bias.
proposals.method: We will re-analyze existing LEAP datasets (e.g., LEAP-ASIA-2019) from multiple facilities, including Kyoto University's centrifuge data. A framework will be developed to extract a correlated signature from synchronized measurements: acceleration time histories (spectral analysis), pore pressures (dissipation patterns), soil displacement fields (via PIV analysis for shear strain localization), and derived stress paths. The core analysis will use multivariate statistical techniques, specifically Principal Component Analysis (PCA) and clustering algorithms (e.g., k-means), to group facility-specific response patterns. The resulting signatures will be correlated with documented differences in model preparation (e.g., pluviation method, saturation technique) and boundary conditions (e.g., laminar box properties).
proposals.contribution: This work will provide the community with a diagnostic framework to quantitatively assess and attribute centrifuge test discrepancies. It will produce specific, actionable guidelines for model preparation and instrumentation to minimize facility-specific bias. Ultimately, it will enhance the reliability of physical modeling data used for calibrating constitutive models and validating numerical simulations of liquefaction.
proposals.novelty: While past studies have compared single metrics like displacement, this is the first to propose a holistic, multi-metric signature analysis to diagnose the *source* of inter-facility bias. The synergy of correlating diverse response measures (acceleration, strain, stress, energy) transforms observed discrepancies from a problem into a tool for protocol validation.

proposals.title: Quantifying the Sources of Inter-Facility Variability in Centrifuge Modeling of Liquefaction-Induced Deformations
proposals.motivation: Large-scale collaborative projects like LEAP have revealed significant, unexplained differences in centrifuge test results for identical model specifications across different facilities. This variability undermines confidence in centrifuge modeling as a reliable validation tool for numerical codes and hinders the standardization of physical modeling for geotechnical earthquake engineering. Resolving these discrepancies is critical for advancing the use of centrifuge data in performance-based design and liquefaction hazard assessment.
proposals.hypothesis: The primary causes of inter-facility result differences are systematic variations in (1) the preparation and placement of saturated sandy soil models, leading to differing initial fabric and stress states, and (2) the boundary conditions and input motion characteristics of the shaking tables, rather than random experimental error.
proposals.method: We will conduct a meta-analysis of all publicly available LEAP (2017, 2019) centrifuge test data, focusing on pore pressure response and lateral displacement. A controlled, round-robin experimental program will be designed where identical, characterized Ottawa sand is distributed to participating facilities (including Kyoto University). We will enforce a strict, step-by-step model construction protocol and employ high-resolution in-flight CPT and bender elements to quantify initial state differences. Time-series data will be analyzed using system identification techniques to deconvolve facility-specific transfer functions of the shaking table and container system.
proposals.contribution: This work will produce the first quantified framework for disaggregating the sources of inter-facility variability in centrifuge testing.
