It will yield practical guidelines for model preparation and quality control to improve reproducibility. The findings will strengthen the foundation of physical modeling for validating constitutive models and numerical simulations used in liquefaction prediction.
proposals.novelty: While LEAP comparisons highlight variability, no study has systematically designed and executed a controlled experiment to isolate and rank the causative factors, combining meta-analysis with a prescribed round-robin test using shared materials and advanced in-situ characterization.

proposals.title: Physics-Informed Discovery of Generalized Scaling Laws for Liquefaction-Induced Slope Deformation via Dimensional Analysis and Neural Networks
proposals.motivation: Scaling laws derived from centrifuge tests are critical for predicting prototype-scale geotechnical behavior, but current empirical laws are often limited to specific conditions and lack rigorous physical constraints. The LEAP projects have highlighted the variability in model tests and the need for robust, generalizable scaling relationships, particularly for seismic slope stability and liquefaction-induced lateral spreading. A physics-constrained, data-driven framework is required to unify these results and provide reliable extrapolation for design.
proposals.hypothesis: That a Physics-Informed Neural Network (PINN), trained on a dimensionless parameter space defined by the Buckingham Pi theorem, can discover explicit, physically consistent functional forms for scaling laws that accurately predict centrifuge slope response, including deformation and pore pressure, beyond its training dataset.
proposals.method: First, the Buckingham Pi theorem will be applied to slope geometry, soil properties (permeability, shear modulus), and dynamic loading to define the complete set of governing dimensionless Pi groups. A neural network (e.g., a multi-layer perceptron) will then be trained to map these input Pi groups to output Pi groups representing normalized deformation. The loss function will combine data misfit from high-quality centrifuge datasets (e.g., LEAP-UCD-2017, LEAP-ASIA-2019) with physics-based penalty terms enforcing known asymptotic behaviors and dimensional homogeneity, using automatic differentiation for constraint implementation.
proposals.contribution: This work will produce a validated, explicit scaling law model with quantified uncertainty, directly useful for centrifuge test interpretation and prototype prediction. It will provide a novel methodology to synthesize disparate experimental results from international centrifuge programs into a unified, physics-consistent framework, advancing the reliability of physical modeling in geotechnical earthquake engineering.
proposals.novelty: The integration of rigorous dimensional analysis as a pre-processing step for a PINN to discover scaling laws is novel in geotechnical engineering. This moves beyond purely empirical curve-fitting or purely numerical simulation by embedding first-principles physical constraints directly into a machine learning model designed for extrapolation.

proposals.title: A Dimensional Analysis Framework for Centrifuge Slope Modelling: Deriving Generalized Scaling Laws via the Buckingham Pi Theorem
proposals.motivation: Geotechnical centrifuge modelling is essential for studying soil liquefaction and slope stability, but scaling laws for complex, non-linear soil behavior under seismic loading remain partially empirical. Projects like LEAP-ASIA-2019 reveal discrepancies between different centrifuge facilities, highlighting the need for a more rigorous, physics-based scaling framework. A systematic derivation of dimensionless governing groups would enhance the reliability and interpretability of centrifuge test data for engineering design.
proposals.hypothesis: The Buckingham Pi theorem can be systematically applied to the centrifuge slope system to derive a complete set of dimensionless Pi terms that govern its static and dynamic response. These derived scaling laws will formally reconcile model and prototype behavior, providing a theoretical basis beyond traditional centrifuge scaling which primarily relies on Froude scaling for inertial effects.
proposals.method: First, we will define the system by listing all relevant physical variables: angular velocity (ω), gravitational acceleration (g), characteristic length (L), soil density (ρ), shear modulus (G), cohesion (c), friction angle (φ), permeability (k), and viscosity of pore fluid (μ). Applying the Buckingham Pi theorem with fundamental dimensions (M, L, T) will yield the complete set of independent dimensionless groups (e.g., ω²L/g, c/ρω²L², G/ρω²L², kρω/μ). We will then validate these groups by re-analyzing existing centrifuge test data from LEAP-UCD-2017 and LEAP-ASIA-2019, assessing whether the derived Pi terms successfully collapse response data (e.g., lateral displacement) across different scaling factors and model configurations.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide a formally derived, generalized set of scaling laws for centrifuge modelling of slopes, explicitly linking geotechnical material properties to the scaling process. It will offer a clear protocol for designing centrifuge tests and a robust framework for interpreting results, directly addressing validation challenges highlighted in international benchmark studies. The outcome will be a significant step towards standardizing centrifuge testing practices.
proposals.novelty: The novelty lies in the direct adaptation of a formal dimensional analysis technique, commonly used in astrophysics and fluid mechanics, to the foundational scaling problem in geotechnical centrifuge modelling. While the Buckingham Pi theorem is known, its rigorous application to derive all governing groups for a centrifuged soil slope—integrating strength, stiffness, and seepage effects—is novel and moves beyond the established, often incomplete, empirical scaling approaches.

proposals.title: A Dimensional Analysis Framework for Scaling Laws in Centrifuge Modeling of Seismic Slope Response and Liquefaction
proposals.motivation: Centrifuge modeling is a cornerstone of geotechnical earthquake engineering, but scaling laws for complex, coupled phenomena like seismic slope failure and liquefaction-induced deformation are often empirically derived or based on incomplete similitude. This can lead to uncertainties when extrapolating model results to prototype-scale predictions. A rigorous, physics-based scaling framework is needed to improve the reliability of centrifuge tests for assessing seismic slope stability.
proposals.hypothesis: The Buckingham Pi theorem can be systematically applied to the governing equations of dynamic soil-fluid interaction in a sloping deposit to derive a complete set of dimensionless Pi terms, which will reveal previously overlooked scaling conflicts and provide a validated framework for correlating centrifuge model behavior with prototype response.
proposals.method: The research will first formulate the complete physical problem, identifying all relevant variables (e.g., soil density, permeability, shear modulus, cyclic strength, seismic acceleration, frequency, slope angle, gravity). The Buckingham Pi theorem will be applied to this variable set to derive the fundamental dimensionless groups. These Pi terms will be compared to existing generalized scaling laws (e.g., from LEAP projects) and used to re-analyze data from past centrifuge tests (e.g., LEAP-UCD-2017, LEAP-ASIA-2019). The analysis will identify which terms are perfectly scaled, which are distorted, and quantify the resulting errors in model response.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide a transparent, first-principles derivation of scaling laws for seismic slope problems, clarifying the limitations of current centrifuge practice. It will yield a practical methodology for designing future centrifuge tests and correcting model data, thereby enhancing the predictive power of physical modeling for liquefaction and slope stability assessment.
proposals.novelty: While dimensional analysis is common in fluid mechanics, its rigorous, comprehensive application to the coupled solid-fluid dynamics of seismic slope failure in centrifuge models is novel. This approach moves beyond the standard Iai-type scaling to explicitly address conflicts in scaling permeability, dilation, and seismic wave frequency simultaneously.

proposals.title: Multi-Fidelity Validation of Seismic Slope Stability Models: Integrating Full-Scale Monitoring, Constructed Field Experiments, and Centrifuge Tests
proposals.motivation: Current validation of geotechnical models for slope stability, particularly under seismic loading and liquefaction, relies heavily on centrifuge tests and limited case histories. This creates a scalability gap, as it is unclear if mechanisms observed in small-scale models accurately represent complex, full-scale field behavior under real environmental forcing. A robust, multi-tiered validation framework is needed to improve the predictive reliability of numerical models used in critical infrastructure design.
proposals.hypothesis: That a hybrid validation framework, combining data from intensively instrumented natural slopes, controlled intermediate-scale field experiments, and centrifuge models, can systematically distinguish fundamental, scalable physical mechanisms from scale-dependent or site-specific artifacts in slope failure predictions.
proposals.method: The framework establishes three data tiers: 1) A core of 2-3 fully instrumented natural slopes (monitoring pore pressure, deformation, seismic response), 2) A network of 10-15 lower-cost monitoring sites for spatial context, and 3) Parallel, constructed intermediate-scale (5-10m high) field slopes where failure is artificially induced via controlled infiltration or a portable shaker. Data from all tiers, including documented case histories, will be used as validation targets for high-fidelity finite element models (e.g., OpenSees with PM4Sand/PM4Silt). The scalability of centrifuge-derived trends, especially for liquefaction-induced deformation, will be tested by comparing model predictions against the multi-fidelity field data.
proposals.contribution: This work will produce a first-of-its-kind, open-access validation dataset spanning multiple physical scales for slope stability. It will provide a rigorous benchmark for calibrating constitutive models and evaluating predictive uncertainty. The results will directly inform the extrapolation of centrifuge findings, like those from LEAP, to field-scale engineering practice.
proposals.novelty: The novelty lies in the structured integration of controlled, destructible field experiments with passive natural slope monitoring to actively challenge the scalability of centrifuge models, moving beyond simple case history comparison to a physics-based validation of scaling laws.

proposals.title: Full-Scale Field Validation of Centrifuge-Derived Scaling Laws for Liquefaction-Induced Deformation in Natural Slopes
proposals.motivation: Centrifuge modeling, as demonstrated in LEAP projects, is a cornerstone of geotechnical earthquake engineering for predicting soil liquefaction and lateral spreading. However, the validation of these models has been confined to the centrifuge environment itself or to limited, often reconstituted, field case histories. A critical gap exists in directly testing the generalized scaling laws and model predictions against controlled, instrumented, full-scale natural slopes subjected to known seismic loading, which is essential for improving the reliability of hazard assessments for infrastructure.
proposals.hypothesis: The deformation mechanisms and magnitude of liquefaction-induced lateral spreading observed in centrifuge models of homogeneous and layered slopes will scale predictably to full-scale natural slopes when subjected to equivalent seismic loading, as defined by the generalized scaling law used in LEAP projects.
proposals.method: We will identify a suitable natural, gently sloping deposit of loose, saturated sandy soil. The site will be extensively instrumented with accelerometer arrays, pore pressure transducers, and displacement sensors (e.g., ShapeAccelArrays). A controlled, high-energy seismic source (e.g., a large-scale vibroseis truck or blast-induced ground motion) will be used to generate strong ground motions with known input characteristics. The dynamic response—including acceleration time histories, excess pore pressure generation, and permanent lateral displacement—will be recorded and compared directly to predictions from prior centrifuge tests (e.g., LEAP-UCD-2017, LEAP-ASIA-2019) by applying the same generalized scaling law in reverse to upscale the model data.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide the first high-quality, fully instrumented dataset of a triggered full-scale lateral spread under known boundary conditions and input motion. It will directly test the validity of centrifuge scaling laws for liquefaction problems in a field setting, moving beyond model-to-model validation. The results will significantly strengthen confidence in using centrifuge-derived design relationships for practical seismic performance assessment of slopes and embankments.
proposals.novelty: While centrifuge model tests are routinely validated against other centrifuge tests, this proposal pioneers a controlled, source-to-response validation at full scale in a natural soil deposit, creating a crucial missing link in the verification chain for physical modeling of liquefaction.

proposals.title: High-Throughput Screening of Seismic Soil-Structure Interaction Using a Microfluidic-Inspired 1-g Shaking Platform
proposals.motivation: Understanding seismic soil-structure interaction (SSI) requires exploring a vast parameter space of soil properties, ground motions, and structural geometries, which is prohibitively expensive and time-consuming with large-scale centrifuge or shake-table tests. This bottleneck limits the statistical validation of numerical models and the identification of critical, non-intuitive failure scenarios. A rapid, low-cost screening method is needed to guide targeted, high-fidelity experimental campaigns like LEAP.
proposals.hypothesis: A microfluidic-inspired, parallelized 1-g shaking platform using micro-granular materials and microfabricated models can efficiently map statistical trends in seismic response, accurately identifying key parameter combinations that lead to amplified settlement, liquefaction, or structural distress.
proposals.method: We will develop a benchtop array of small, instrumented soil chambers (e.g., 5x5 cm) containing uniform or layered deposits of fine silica flour or glass beads (75-150 μm) to model saturated granular soils. Micro-milled or 3D-printed structural elements (e.g., pile groups, shallow foundations) will be embedded. The platform will use a programmable, high-precision linear actuator to apply scaled historical ground motions. A machine vision system (high-speed camera with digital image correlation) will automate measurement of soil deformation, pore pressure analog (via transparent sidewall imaging), and structural displacement. Thousands of tests will be run by systematically varying input motion (amplitude, frequency), soil density, and model geometry.
proposals.contribution: This work will produce a novel, open-source experimental methodology for rapid seismic SSI parameter screening. It will generate extensive benchmark datasets of statistical performance trends, providing a crucial 'first pass' to inform and optimize the design of subsequent centrifuge tests. The approach will also offer a physical validation tool for discrete element method (DEM) simulations at the particle scale.
proposals.novelty: The novelty lies in adapting the high-throughput, lab-on-a-chip philosophy from microfluidics to geotechnical earthquake engineering, enabling a paradigm shift from few, expensive tests to many, inexpensive, automated experiments. This systematic screening approach is distinct from traditional physical modeling aimed at direct prototype simulation.

proposals.title: High-Throughput 1-g Shaking Table Screening of Seepage-Managing Granular Designs for Seismic Pore Pressure Dissipation
proposals.motivation: Liquefaction mitigation often relies on rigid, impermeable barriers or dense compaction, which can be costly and difficult to implement. A paradigm shift towards managing, rather than blocking, seismic seepage flow offers potential for more adaptable and economical solutions. However, the complex, coupled hydro-mechanical performance of such designs under dynamic loading is poorly understood, creating a barrier to practical application.
proposals.hypothesis: That granular designs featuring engineered internal drainage pathways (e.g., high-permeability lenses, sacrificial zones, or controlled permeability contrasts) can be systematically optimized via physical testing to effectively dissipate seismic pore pressures and mitigate liquefaction-induced deformations in sloping or layered ground.
proposals.method: We will employ a modular, low-cost 1-g shaking table platform to conduct high-throughput tests on small-scale soil models. A factorial experimental design will screen variables like drain geometry, grain size distribution contrasts, and layer inclination. Models, instrumented with pore pressure transducers and accelerometers, will be subjected to scaled earthquake motions. Performance will be quantified by metrics of excess pore pressure ratio reduction and lateral displacement, enabling rapid empirical correlation of design parameters to stability outcomes.
proposals.contribution: This work will produce a first-of-its-kind empirical database linking specific seepage-management design parameters to dynamic performance. It will yield practical, granular-based design principles for liquefaction mitigation that complement existing methods. The findings will provide a crucial physical validation step before costly centrifuge testing or field trials, accelerating the development of implementable solutions.
proposals.novelty: The fusion of the 'manage seepage' paradigm with a high-throughput, iterative physical screening methodology is novel. It moves beyond validating single designs to rapidly exploring a wide, non-intuitive design space for seismic drainage, a approach not yet systematized in geotechnical engineering practice pre-2026.

proposals.title: Inverse Characterization of Heterogeneous Soil Profiles via Centrifuge Data Fusion for Advanced Site Response Prediction
proposals.motivation: Current practice for site response analysis often relies on simplified, homogeneous soil models, which fail to capture the complex stratigraphy and property gradients (e.g., silty sand layers) prevalent in real deposits. This simplification introduces significant uncertainty in predicting liquefaction triggering and lateral spreading, as evidenced by the scatter in LEAP project validation exercises. A robust method to inversely characterize spatially varying soil parameters from dynamic response data is therefore critical for accurate, site-specific seismic performance assessment.
proposals.hypothesis: That spatially varying soil constitutive parameters (e.g., shear modulus reduction and damping curves, permeability) within a heterogeneous profile can be reliably back-calculated by fusing high-fidelity centrifuge test data with a layered finite element model through an inverse analysis framework, leading to significantly improved predictions of site-specific dynamic response.
proposals.method: The methodology will leverage Dr. Tobita's expertise in centrifuge modeling (e.g., LEAP-ASIA-2019) to generate benchmark data from instrumented models with controlled, layered heterogeneity (e.g., a sloping deposit with a low-permeability silt layer). An inverse analysis framework will be developed, coupling a layered soil constitutive model (e.g., a multi-yield surface plasticity model) with a gradient-based optimization algorithm (e.g., the Levenberg-Marquardt algorithm) or a Bayesian updating scheme. The algorithm will iteratively adjust layer-specific parameters to minimize the discrepancy between simulated and measured time histories of acceleration, displacement, and pore pressure.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide a validated, practical framework for deriving high-resolution, site-specific soil models from field or experimental data, moving beyond bulk property estimation. It will directly contribute to more reliable predictions of liquefaction-induced deformations in complex geological settings. The resulting methodology will be a transformative tool for geotechnical earthquake engineering practice and risk assessment.
proposals.novelty: While inverse analysis for soil properties exists, its application to simultaneously back-calculate property gradients and layer interfaces from fully coupled dynamic response (stress-strain-pore pressure) in heterogeneous systems is novel. This fusion of stratigraphic complexity with multi-parameter inversion specifically targets the limitations revealed by recent international centrifuge validation projects.

proposals.title: Quantitative Assessment of Frozen Sample Integrity for Cyclic Liquefaction Testing: A Centrifuge and Element Test Validation Study
proposals.motivation: The frozen sampling technique is widely used to obtain 'undisturbed' samples of sandy soils for advanced laboratory testing, particularly for evaluating seismic liquefaction potential. However, its efficacy in preserving the in-situ soil fabric, particle arrangement, and effective stress state—critical factors controlling cyclic strength—remains a significant, unresolved question in geotechnical engineering. Validating this method is essential for ensuring the reliability of laboratory-derived liquefaction assessments used in critical infrastructure design.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that the freezing process and subsequent thawing in the laboratory induce subtle but consequential alterations to the soil fabric and stress memory, leading to a systematic overestimation or underestimation of the cyclic resistance ratio (CRR) when compared to the true in-situ state.
proposals.method: The research will employ a comprehensive centrifuge modeling approach at Kyoto University. A uniform sand deposit will be prepared and consolidated to a known state in the centrifuge, representing a well-controlled 'in-situ' condition. A section will be frozen in-flight under the target centrifugal stress field. Post-test, frozen samples will be extracted, thawed, and subjected to cyclic triaxial and simple shear tests. Their cyclic resistance will be directly compared to identical tests performed on reconstituted samples created at the same density and stress state, and to the observed liquefaction triggering in the parent centrifuge model during simulated seismic shaking.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide the first direct, quantitative evidence linking the frozen sampling process to measurable changes in cyclic strength. It will deliver a crucial validation or calibration framework for interpreting laboratory data from frozen samples, directly impacting the practice of seismic ground response analysis and liquefaction hazard evaluation for important projects.
proposals.novelty: While frozen sampling is common, this study uniquely uses centrifuge modeling to create a verifiable in-situ benchmark, allowing for a direct, paired comparison between the true in-situ behavior and the behavior of the frozen-and-thawed 'undisturbed' sample derived from it, a validation not possible in the field.

proposals.title: Validation and Calibration of a Multispring Model for Liquefaction Simulation of Silty Sands and Non-Plastic Silts Using Centrifuge Test Data
proposals.motivation: The multispring (MS) model is a promising constitutive model for simulating soil liquefaction and cyclic degradation in effective stress analysis. However, its validation has been largely confined to clean sands, limiting its predictive reliability for the silty and low-plasticity soils frequently encountered in geotechnical engineering projects. A systematic validation against high-quality experimental data for these common soil types is needed to enhance the model's practical utility in seismic design and risk assessment.
proposals.hypothesis: The multispring model can be accurately calibrated to replicate the cyclic strength, pore pressure generation, and post-liquefaction deformation characteristics of sands with varying fines content and non-plastic silts, provided its parameters are systematically linked to index properties and state variables.
proposals.method: We will calibrate and validate the MS model using existing centrifuge test data from the LEAP projects and other published datasets for Ottawa sand, silica sand with fines, and Toyoura sand with silt. The model parameters, particularly those governing the backbone curve, hardening, and cyclic degradation, will be inversely determined via optimization algorithms (e.g., genetic algorithm or Bayesian updating) to match recorded accelerations, pore pressures, and displacements. The calibrated model will then be used in finite element simulations of the centrifuge models to assess its predictive capability for different seismic intensities and soil densities.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide a validated set of MS model parameters for a broader range of soils, directly increasing its applicability in engineering practice. It will establish clearer guidelines for parameter selection based on soil classification, advancing the standardization of performance-based seismic design for liquefiable ground.
proposals.novelty: While MS model applications exist, a focused, soil-type-specific validation campaign linking model parameters directly to fines content and state, and rigorously testing it against benchmark centrifuge data for silty soils, represents a significant step beyond existing calibrations primarily for clean sands.

proposals.title: Machine Learning Prediction of Cyclic Resistance Ratio Curves from Soil Index Properties Using a Hybrid Database of Laboratory and Centrifuge Tests
proposals.motivation: Predicting soil liquefaction resistance, characterized by the cyclic resistance ratio (CRR) curve, is critical for seismic geotechnical engineering. Current empirical methods based on in-situ tests like SPT or CPT are well-established but require specialized site investigation. A model predicting the full CRR curve from basic index properties (e.g., fines content, plasticity index, relative density) would enable rapid preliminary assessment, especially for excavated or reconstituted soils common in earthworks. This addresses a gap between fundamental soil behavior and practical engineering screening tools.
proposals.hypothesis: Machine learning models can accurately predict the shape and position of the cyclic resistance ratio (CRR) versus number of cycles (N) curve for sandy and silty soils using a limited set of soil index properties and initial state parameters. The model's performance will be enhanced by training on a hybrid database combining high-quality element test data with validated centrifuge model test results.
proposals.method: We will compile a curated database from published literature and internal Kyoto University data, including index properties (grain size distribution, fines content, plasticity) and corresponding CRR curves from cyclic triaxial/torsional shear tests. This will be augmented with data from well-instrumented centrifuge model tests (e.g., from LEAP projects), using scaling laws to derive equivalent element-level stress-strain responses. Feature engineering will include state indices like state parameter. We will test and compare several ML algorithms, including Gradient Boosting Machines (XGBoost/LightGBM) for robust regression and Multi-Layer Perceptrons (MLPs), to predict key curve parameters or the full curve discretized at standard cycle numbers.
proposals.contribution: This research will produce a novel, data-driven tool for estimating liquefaction resistance curves from readily available soil data, complementing existing in-situ test-based methods. It will demonstrate the value of integrating physical model test data (centrifuge) with laboratory element test data to create more generalizable ML models in geotechnics. The resulting model will be a practical contribution to preliminary seismic hazard assessment and digital soil modeling workflows.
proposals.novelty: While ML has been applied to binary liquefaction triggering, predicting the entire CRR curve from index properties is unexplored. The innovative fusion of high-quality laboratory test data with systematically scaled centrifuge model test results to train the ML model represents a novel hybrid data approach that leverages the researcher's unique expertise in centrifuge modeling of liquefaction.

proposals.title: A Probabilistic Liquefaction Hazard Map for the Osaka Basin Integrating Site-Specific Centrifuge-Derived Resistance with Regional Seismic Hazard Analysis
proposals.motivation: Current regional liquefaction hazard maps in Japan often rely on simplified empirical correlations (e.g., SPT-N values) and generic soil classifications, which may not capture the complex, spatially variable dynamic response of alluvial deposits under strong shaking. The densely populated and economically vital Osaka region, underlain by thick, loose alluvial soils, requires a more physically robust and site-specific hazard assessment to inform resilient urban planning and infrastructure design. This need is amplified by the probabilistic seismic hazard models (PSHA) for the Nankai Trough megathrust earthquake, which indicate high ground motion probabilities for the region.
proposals.hypothesis: Integrating site-specific cyclic resistance ratios (CRR) derived from validated centrifuge model tests and generalized scaling laws into a probabilistic framework will yield a regional liquefaction hazard map with significantly reduced epistemic uncertainty compared to maps based solely on index properties.
proposals.method: The research will first compile a high-resolution geotechnical database for the Osaka basin, synthesizing borehole data, soil profiles, and groundwater levels. Site-specific CRR curves for representative soil profiles will be developed using the generalized scaling laws validated in LEAP projects, informed by Tobita's prior centrifuge tests on sloping ground and lateral spreading. These CRR relationships will be incorporated into a Monte Carlo-based probabilistic seismic hazard analysis (PSHA) framework. The analysis will use existing seismic source models and ground motion prediction equations for Japan to compute the annual rate of exceeding critical CRR thresholds, generating probabilistic liquefaction hazard curves and maps at a 250m grid resolution.
proposals.contribution: This work will produce the first probabilistic liquefaction hazard map for Osaka that directly integrates physics-based, centrifuge-validated soil resistance models. It will provide a quantitative assessment of spatial liquefaction risk, crucial for prioritizing ground improvement and retrofitting. The methodology will establish a transferable template for upgrading regional hazard maps in other seismically active alluvial basins worldwide.
proposals.novelty: The novelty lies in the direct upscaling of detailed, mechanism-focused centrifuge test results—typically used for site-specific validation—to inform a regional-scale probabilistic hazard model, creating a critical bridge between physical model testing and practical risk assessment at the city scale.

proposals.title: Centrifuge Investigation of Post-Liquefaction Strength Recovery Mechanisms in Silty Sands: Rate, Path-Dependency, and Implications for Permanent Displacement
proposals.motivation: While extensive research has characterized soil strength loss during liquefaction, the subsequent recovery of strength and stiffness—critical for assessing permanent displacements and the performance of structures and slopes after shaking—remains poorly quantified. Current engineering practice often uses simplistic, non-time-dependent post-liquefaction strength parameters, which may lead to inaccurate predictions of post-earthquake stability and deformation. A systematic study of this 'dual problem' is essential for advancing performance-based earthquake engineering.
proposals.hypothesis: The rate and final magnitude of post-liquefaction strength recovery in silty sands are governed by a combination of soil fabric reformation, dissipation of excess pore pressure, and the re-establishment of effective stress, and this recovery is path-dependent on the initial shear strain induced during liquefaction.
proposals.method: The research will employ a series of centrifuge model tests at Kyoto University's facility, building on the LEAP experimental framework. Saturated model slopes of silty sand will be subjected to controlled base shaking to induce liquefaction and lateral spreading. Post-shaking, the models will be subjected to staged, in-flight static loading tests (e.g., using a model footing or a controlled basal movement) at precise time intervals after shaking cessation. Pore pressure transducers, accelerometers, and displacement sensors will monitor the dissipation of excess pore pressure and the corresponding regain in load-bearing capacity. The data will be analyzed to develop a time-strain-strength relationship.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide the first comprehensive, experimentally derived framework for quantifying the time-dependent regain of shear strength in liquefied silty sands. It will deliver crucial data for calibrating and validating advanced constitutive models that simulate post-liquefaction behavior. The findings will directly inform improved guidelines for estimating permanent displacements in slopes and foundations following seismic events.
proposals.novelty: This proposal shifts the focus from the well-studied loss of strength *during* liquefaction to its systematic recovery *after* shaking, a critical but underexplored phase. It uniquely combines the high-gravity realism of centrifuge modeling with controlled post-liquefaction strength probing to directly measure recovery as a function of time and prior strain history.

proposals.title: High-Resolution Centrifuge Modeling of Cyclic Pore Pressure Buildup and Dissipation in Stratified Sand Layers under Seismic Loading
proposals.motivation: Predicting soil liquefaction and its consequences, such as lateral spreading, remains a critical challenge in geotechnical earthquake engineering. While centrifuge modeling has validated macro-scale deformations, the detailed, transient pore pressure response within thin, vulnerable sand layers embedded in stratified deposits is poorly understood. This gap limits the accuracy of constitutive models and performance-based design for critical infrastructure.
proposals.hypothesis: The spatiotemporal evolution of pore pressure in stratified soil systems during and after seismic shaking is governed by a complex interplay of cyclic stress ratio, layer thickness, permeability contrasts, and drainage boundary conditions, which can be uniquely captured through instrumented physical modeling.
proposals.method: We will conduct a series of dynamic centrifuge tests at Kyoto University, building on the LEAP experimental framework. A stratified model with instrumented, saturated sand layers of varying thickness and density will be subjected to scaled earthquake motions. High-density miniature pore pressure transducers (PPTs) and particle image velocimetry (PIV) will be deployed to capture the generation, redistribution, and dissipation of pore water pressure at the layer scale. Data will be analyzed to develop and calibrate a coupled, effective-stress finite element model using a multi-surface plasticity constitutive law (e.g., PM4Sand) to back-analyze the tests.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide a unique, high-resolution dataset of in-layer pore pressure dynamics for validating and improving numerical constitutive models. It will yield practical insights into the conditions leading to delayed or partial liquefaction in stratified profiles, directly informing the assessment of liquefaction-induced deformations for dams and slopes.
proposals.novelty: Unlike previous centrifuge studies focusing on global response or homogeneous layers, this proposal targets the meso-scale pore pressure mechanisms within identified, thin vulnerable strata, integrating advanced physical modeling with high-fidelity sensor data for detailed model calibration.

proposals.title: Inverse Estimation of Historical Seismic Shaking from Paleoliquefaction Evidence Using a Centrifuge-Validated Bayesian Framework
proposals.motivation: Paleoliquefaction features are critical for extending seismic hazard records beyond instrumental and historical archives, especially in regions with long recurrence intervals. However, current methods for back-calculating causative earthquake parameters (e.g., magnitude, distance, peak ground acceleration) rely on simplified empirical correlations with significant uncertainty. A more rigorous, physics-based inverse framework is needed to provide reliable constraints for probabilistic seismic hazard analysis (PSHA).
proposals.hypothesis: The characteristics of a historical earthquake (magnitude and epicentral distance) can be probabilistically constrained by inverting field-observed liquefaction manifestations through a numerical model whose parameters and predictive uncertainty are rigorously calibrated against high-fidelity centrifuge test data.
proposals.method: The research will employ a Bayesian inverse analysis framework. First, a database of centrifuge model tests (leveraging LEAP project data) on liquefaction and lateral spreading in sloping ground will be used to calibrate and validate a constitutive soil model (e.g., PM4Sand) within a finite difference code. This calibration will quantify model prediction uncertainty. This validated forward model will then be used within a Markov Chain Monte Carlo (MCMC) algorithm to solve the inverse problem: given field observations of liquefaction extent and lateral displacement from a paleoseismic trench, the posterior probability distributions of the causative shaking intensity (PGA) and duration will be inferred. These shaking parameters will then be related to earthquake magnitude and distance via ground motion models.
proposals.contribution: This work will deliver a novel, probabilistically rigorous methodology for paleoseismic source characterization, directly integrating physical modeling validation. It will advance the field from empirical 'back-calculation' to a formal inverse problem framework with quantified uncertainty, providing more reliable inputs for seismic hazard models. The centrifuge-calibrated forward model ensures the inversion is grounded in realistic soil behavior.
proposals.novelty: While forward modeling of liquefaction is common, the formal application of a Bayesian inverse framework, explicitly informed and constrained by high-quality centrifuge experimental data, to paleoliquefaction problems is a significant methodological advance beyond existing empirical charts and deterministic procedures.

proposals.title: Scaling Laws for Centrifuge Modeling of Layered Soils with Depth-Dependent Density: Validation via LEAP-Type Benchmark Experiments
proposals.motivation: Centrifuge modeling is a cornerstone of geotechnical earthquake engineering, but its scaling laws are typically derived assuming constant soil density. In reality, soil density often increases significantly with depth due to overburden pressure, affecting stiffness, permeability, and dynamic response. This discrepancy may introduce critical scaling errors when modeling phenomena like liquefaction-induced lateral spreading in layered deposits, limiting the predictive accuracy of physical models for real-world, non-homogeneous sites.
proposals.hypothesis: The Generalized Scaling Law (GSL) for dynamic centrifuge modeling requires modification to account for significant, systematic density gradients with depth, and failure to do so leads to non-conservable scaling of key seismic response parameters such as excess pore pressure generation and lateral displacement.
proposals.method: We will conduct a series of centrifuge tests at Kyoto University, building on the LEAP-UCD-2017/2019 framework. A sloping ground model will be constructed with controlled, non-homogeneous layers of Ottawa F-65 sand, achieving a target density profile (e.g., loose at surface, dense at base). Dynamic excitations will be applied. The experimental results will be compared directly to (1) a prototype-scale numerical model and (2) a centrifuge-scale numerical model that uses standard GSL scaling of a dense-equivalent homogeneous soil. The discrepancy will be analyzed to propose and validate a modified scaling approach for density gradients, implemented through dimensional analysis and targeted finite element simulations using constitutive models like PM4Sand.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide a validated, practical extension to the Generalized Scaling Law for centrifuge modeling of common non-homogeneous soil deposits. It will deliver a new benchmark dataset for the LEAP community to calibrate numerical models against layered systems. The outcome will improve the reliability of physical modeling for seismic performance assessment of infrastructure on natural, layered soil foundations.
proposals.novelty: While LEAP studies have validated scaling for uniform and mildly layered deposits, a systematic investigation targeting *prescribed, significant density gradients* as the primary variable is novel. This moves beyond validating existing laws to explicitly challenging and refining a fundamental assumption within them.

proposals.title: Inverse Application of the Generalized Scaling Law for Predicting Centrifuge Model Behavior via 1g Small-Scale Physical Tests
proposals.motivation: Centrifuge testing is a cornerstone of geotechnical earthquake engineering for simulating prototype stress conditions, but it is expensive, time-consuming, and requires specialized facilities. Conversely, 1g small-scale tests are far more accessible but are traditionally considered non-representative due to scaling conflicts. A systematic method to design 1g tests that can reliably predict centrifuge outcomes would democratize advanced physical modeling, enabling wider validation of numerical models and rapid parametric studies.
proposals.hypothesis: The Generalized Scaling Law (GSL), previously used to relate centrifuge models to prototypes, can be inverted to design 1g small-scale models whose measured response (e.g., deformation, pore pressure) will directly predict the response of an equivalent centrifuge model when scaled up according to the same law.
proposals.method: The research will employ a three-phase experimental and analytical approach. First, select a well-documented centrifuge test case (e.g., a LEAP-UCD-2017 liquefiable slope model) as the target. Second, invert the GSL to derive the required geometry, soil properties, and input motion for a 1g small-scale model. Third, construct and test this designed 1g model using a small shaking table. The dynamic response (accelerations, displacements, excess pore pressures) will be recorded and then scaled up using the GSL to produce a prediction of the centrifuge model's response, which will be quantitatively compared to the actual centrifuge data.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide a validated, practical framework for using inexpensive 1g physical tests as predictive tools for centrifuge-level problems, specifically for liquefaction-induced deformations. It will extend the utility of the GSL from a centrifuge scaling tool to a design law for accessible experimentation, potentially enabling more universities and labs to contribute to high-fidelity physical modeling research.
proposals.novelty: While the GSL is established for centrifuge-to-prototype scaling, its deliberate inversion to design predictive 1g models is a novel, non-obvious application. This inverts the traditional hierarchy of physical modeling, aiming to make the 1g test not a simplified analogue but a quantitatively designed precursor to centrifuge testing.

proposals.title: Validation of the Generalized Scaling Law for Liquefaction-Induced Lateral Spreading at High Shear Strain Levels Using Centrifuge Modeling
proposals.motivation: The generalized scaling law (GSL) for centrifuge modeling of liquefaction phenomena, developed and validated through projects like LEAP, is a cornerstone for reliable physical modeling. However, its validation has been primarily confined to moderate shear strain levels (typically up to ~10%). Many real-world failure scenarios, such as severe lateral spreading, flow slides, and post-liquefaction ground deformations near structures, involve significantly higher strain regimes where the scaling relationships for permeability, dilation, and shear band formation remain untested.
proposals.hypothesis: The generalized scaling law holds for shear strain levels significantly exceeding 10%, meaning that centrifuge model results for high-strain liquefaction phenomena (e.g., large lateral displacements, flow failure) will correctly scale to and predict equivalent prototype behavior.
proposals.method: This research will employ a series of centrifuge tests at Kyoto University's facility, building on the established LEAP-ASIA-2019 sloping ground model. We will design new model configurations and seismic input motions to deliberately induce shear strains exceeding 10%, targeting 15-25% levels. The method involves: 1) Using Ottawa F-65 sand prepared to a target relative density with saturation control, 2) Instrumenting models with accelerometers, pore pressure transducers, and high-speed cameras for Particle Image Velocimetry (PIV) to measure soil displacement fields and precise strain localization, and 3) Systematically comparing the observed model-scale stress-strain-time histories and final deformations against the GSL-predicted prototype-scale behavior.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide the first experimental evidence for the validity limits of the GSL under high-strain conditions, a critical gap in current validation efforts. It will enhance confidence in using centrifuge modeling to simulate catastrophic liquefaction failures, directly informing the design of mitigation measures for civil infrastructure and earth structures like dams and levees where large deformations are a concern.
proposals.novelty: While the GSL has been validated for moderate deformations, its applicability to the high-strain regime associated with flow-type failures and complete loss of shear strength is unexplored. This proposal systematically pushes the validated strain boundary, a non-trivial extension given potential changes in soil-fluid coupling and failure mechanisms.

proposals.title: Derivation of Generalized Scaling Laws for Liquefied Soil Flow via Buckingham Pi Theorem and Validation through Centrifuge Modeling
proposals.motivation: Scaling laws for geotechnical centrifuge modeling of liquefaction, such as those used in LEAP projects, are empirically based and often treat soil as a solid with scaling factors for density and viscosity. This approach may not fully capture the fluid-like flow behavior of fully liquefied soil, leading to discrepancies in predicting lateral spreading and its impact on infrastructure. A rigorous similitude framework derived from fluid mechanics principles is needed to improve the predictive accuracy of physical models for post-liquefaction flow.
proposals.hypothesis: The Buckingham Pi theorem can be systematically applied to the governing equations of a two-phase soil-fluid mixture in a liquefied state to derive a consistent set of dimensionless Pi terms. These derived scaling laws will provide more accurate similitude for liquefaction-induced flow phenomena in centrifuge tests compared to conventional scaling approaches.
proposals.method: The research will first define the relevant variables for liquefied soil flow, including soil density, pore fluid viscosity and density, shear strength parameters in a fluidized state, permeability, gravitational acceleration, and a characteristic length and velocity. The Buckingham Pi theorem will be applied to these variables to derive the complete set of dimensionless Pi groups governing the similitude. The derived scaling laws will then be used to design a new series of centrifuge model tests at Kyoto University, focusing on lateral spreading in a sloping ground. The results will be compared against data from previous LEAP experiments (UCD-2017, ASIA-2019) that used conventional scaling, assessing the reduction in discrepancy between model predictions and target prototype behavior.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide a fundamental, mechanics-based scaling framework specifically for the fluid-flow phase of soil liquefaction, moving beyond empirical rules. It will deliver a validated set of scaling relations that can improve the design and interpretation of centrifuge tests for hazards like lateral spreading, thereby enhancing the reliability of physical modeling for seismic risk assessment.
proposals.novelty: While similitude theory is standard in fluid mechanics, its direct and rigorous application to derive scaling laws for the flow of liquefied soil—treating it explicitly as a non-Newtonian fluid mixture—is a novel departure from the standard solid-mechanics-based scaling practices prevalent in geotechnical centrifuge modeling before 2026.

ideas.title: Dimensionally-Aware MPM for Scaling and Predicting Centrifuge Breakwater Collapse
ideas.description: This research fuses the Material Point Method (MPM) for simulating large deformation and collapse with dimensional analysis (Buckingham Pi theorem) to create a novel computational geomechanics framework. The approach uses dimensional analysis to first establish the fundamental non-dimensional Pi groups governing centrifuge slope and breakwater response under extreme loads. These scaling laws are then embedded within the MPM simulation, informing material model parameters, boundary conditions, and failure criteria to ensure the numerical model inherently obeys the correct physical scaling. This synergy allows for the rigorous calibration and validation of the MPM against centrifuge test data at one scale, with the explicit, physics-grounded capability to predict prototype-scale performance and collapse mechanisms, bridging the gap between physical modeling and high-fidelity numerical prediction.

ideas.title: Quantify facility-specific scaling distortions in transient seepage as a primary cause of inter-facility result divergence
ideas.description: This research program directly tests the hypothesis that a major source of inter-facility result differences in centrifuge modeling stems from facility-specific distortions in scaling transient seepage flow through coarse-grained rubble. It will design a standardized benchmark experiment for transient seepage, executed identically across multiple centrifuge facilities, to isolate and measure how differences in model preparation, acceleration ramping, pore fluid properties, and sensor technology interact with and violate the theoretical scaling laws for transient flow. The outcome will be a validated framework for correcting or bounding facility-specific scaling errors, moving the community towards reproducible centrifuge testing of seepage-critical problems.

ideas.title: Apply Buckingham Pi Theorem from Astrophysics to Derive Centrifuge Slope Scaling Laws
ideas.description: This idea adapts the use of dimensional analysis and the Buckingham Pi theorem from astrophysics, specifically from deriving scaling laws for stellar pulsations, to the problem of centrifuge slope response. In astrophysics, this method extracts fundamental relationships (like period-luminosity) from complex physics by identifying governing dimensionless groups. The analogous solution is adapted by defining the centrifuge slope as a system where the equilibrium shape is governed by a balance of centrifugal, gravitational, and material forces. By listing all relevant variables (angular velocity ω, gravity g, characteristic length L, density ρ, material strength parameters, etc.) and applying the Buckingham Pi theorem, we can derive the complete set of dimensionless Pi terms (e.g., a Froude number, a strength-to-stress ratio). These Pi groups form the scaling laws that dictate how slope behavior in a small-scale centrifuge model relates to a full-scale prototype, providing a rigorous foundation for geotechnical centrifuge modelling and removing empirical guesswork.

It will use the high-throughput testing platform to screen a wide array of novel, granular designs—such as engineered drains, permeability contrasts, or sacrificial zones—that aim to safely dissipate seismic pore pressures. The iterative, physical experimentation will reveal non-intuitive performance relationships between design parameters, seepage patterns, and soil stability under dynamic loading. This approach enables the empirical development of new design principles for liquefaction mitigation that are both effective and practical for field implementation.

The core novelty is developing a suite of low-cost, instrumented, programmable flexible containers whose stiffness and damping characteristics can be varied between tests. This setup allows for high-throughput experimental campaigns to systematically decouple and quantify the effects of boundary conditions on measured soil and model structure response during shaking, generating a unique database to validate and improve numerical modeling techniques for boundary effects in physical modeling.

ideas.title: Diagnosing Facility-Specific Bias Through a Multi-Metric Response Signature Framework
ideas.description: This research develops a multi-metric response signature framework to diagnose the root causes of inter-facility discrepancies in centrifuge tests. By analyzing a suite of correlated response metrics—such as acceleration, shear strain localization, stress paths, and system energy, beyond just displacement and pore pressure—it seeks to identify which specific aspects of model preparation, boundary conditions, or instrumentation produce facility-specific biases. The synergy enables the differentiation between true soil behavior variability and systematic experimental error, transforming observed discrepancies into a tool for validating and improving physical modeling protocols across the community.

ideas.title: Physics-Informed ML Correction of 1-g Shaking Table Tests for Prototype-Scale Parameter Exploration
ideas.description: This approach conducts high-volume, low-cost 1-g shaking table tests on small-scale models to rapidly generate a broad dataset of structural and geotechnical system responses. A convolutional neural network or similar ML architecture, pre-trained on a foundational dataset of high-fidelity centrifuge tests and DEM/FEM simulations, is applied to the 1-g test outputs. This model applies a learned distortion-correction transform, accounting for improper stress scaling and granular effects, to predict equivalent full-scale prototype behavior. The result is a hybrid experimental-numerical system that leverages the speed of 1-g testing while providing physically meaningful, prototype-relevant insights across the explored parameter space.

ideas.title: Physics-Informed Machine Learning of Centrifuge Slope Scaling Laws via Dimensional Analysis
ideas.description: This approach first uses the Buckingham Pi theorem to rigorously define the dimensionless parameter space governing centrifuge slope response, ensuring a dimensionally consistent foundation. A physics-informed neural network (PINN) is then trained to discover the functional relationships between these groups, using a limited but high-quality dataset from centrifuge tests or advanced numerical simulations. Physical constraints, such as known asymptotic behaviors or conservation principles, are embedded directly into the loss function to guarantee physically plausible predictions. The final, validated model provides robust, explicit scaling laws that can reliably extrapolate beyond the original training data for geotechnical design and analysis.

ideas.title: A Hybrid Multi-Scale Validation Framework for Slope Stability Models
ideas.description: This redesigned approach creates a tiered validation system to overcome the prohibitive cost and statistical limitations of purely full-scale testing. It establishes a core of intensively instrumented, full-scale natural slopes to capture complex, real-world behavior under actual environmental forcing. This is complemented by a broader network of lower-cost monitoring sites for spatial context and a parallel program of intermediate-scale, constructed field experiments where failure can be artificially induced via controlled infiltration or loading. The integrated data from all three tiers is used to rigorously test the scalability of centrifuge trends, distinguishing fundamental mechanisms from site-specific noise and providing a robust, multi-fidelity validation dataset for predictive models.

ideas.title: Microfluidic Earthquake Simulator for Rapid Parameter Screening
ideas.description: This idea adapts the high-throughput, parameter-screening methodology from microfluidics and lab-on-a-chip technology to the problem of seismic soil-structure interaction. The analogous field solves complex, multi-parameter fluid dynamics problems by constructing scaled-down, parallelized, and automated chip-based testbeds. The adapted research idea involves creating a 'microfluidic earthquake simulator'—a small-scale, 1-g shaking table platform that uses micro-granular materials and microfabricated structural models. This system enables the rapid, low-cost execution of hundreds to thousands of shaking tests, automating data collection on settlement, liquefaction potential, and structural response. The goal is to efficiently explore the vast parameter space of seismic events and soil conditions, generating statistical trends and identifying critical scenarios to guide the design of targeted, large-scale experiments or computational model validation.

ideas.title: Inverse Characterization of Heterogeneous Soil Profiles for Advanced Site Response Prediction
ideas.description: This research develops an inverse analysis methodology to back-calculate spatially varying soil constitutive parameters from experimental or field-monitored dynamic response data (displacement and pore pressure). It specifically targets heterogeneous profiles, such as silty sand layers or stratified deposits, which are common in practice but challenging to characterize. By fusing the inverse problem with complex soil stratigraphy, the work moves beyond estimating bulk properties to identifying property gradients and interfaces critical for predicting liquefaction triggering and manifestation. The resulting calibrated models will provide a transformative tool for assessing site-specific seismic performance where soil uniformity cannot be assumed.

ideas.title: Investigate soil with fines or layered stratigraphy using composite material micromechanics
ideas.description: This research challenges the assumption of clean, uniform sand by testing silty sands or layered soil profiles. It draws an analogy from composite materials science, where micromechanical models (e.g., rule of mixtures, Mori-Tanaka, RVE analysis) successfully predict the macro-scale behavior of heterogeneous materials from the properties and arrangement of their constituents. The adapted idea is to apply this micromechanical framework to soils, explicitly modeling fines or thin layers as distinct phases within a soil matrix. By developing a representative volume element approach calibrated with laboratory and discrete element method simulations, the research aims to create predictive models for the strength, stiffness, and hydraulic conductivity of these complex soils based on their micro-scale composition and structure.

A standardized, instrumented test package will be tested across multiple centrifuge facilities following a common protocol, but with key potential causal factors deliberately and systematically varied in a controlled manner at each site. By comparing results from these orchestrated tests using analysis of variance (ANOVA) techniques, the study will quantify the specific contribution of factors like model preparation, sensor type, shaking protocol, and data processing to the total observed variance, moving from correlation to demonstrated causation.

ideas.title: Graded and Variable Slope Profiles Inspired by Geotechnical Engineering
ideas.description: This idea challenges the assumption of a constant, gentle incline by proposing variable slope angles, drawing an analogy from geotechnical engineering. The objective is to study how phenomena (like material flow, structural stress, or behavioral responses) evolve across these transitions, revealing threshold effects, hysteresis, or localized critical points that are invisible when testing only a single, constant slope angle.

ideas.title: Hybrid Dual-Resolution MPM-FVM Framework for Efficient and Stable Breakwater Collapse Simulation
ideas.description: This research develops a hybrid computational framework to simulate the extreme deformation and collapse of coastal breakwaters by directly addressing the prohibitive cost and numerical noise of a pure Material Point Method (MPM) approach. The core innovation is a dual-domain system that couples a high-resolution MPM simulation for the granular/porous breakwater structure with a more efficient Finite Volume Method (FVM) simulation for the surrounding water and air phases using a momentum-conserving immersed boundary. This design allows the MPM to accurately model fracture, soil liquefaction, and large deformations only where needed, while the FVM handles the fluid dynamics and long-duration wave loading with greater computational efficiency and stability. The result is a robust, engineering-practical tool for simulating complete breakwater failure scenarios from initial wave impact through total collapse.

ideas.title: Data-Driven Selection of Critical Experiments for Full-Field Soil Deformation Analysis via Digital Image Correlation
ideas.description: This research direction begins by using an existing body of literature to identify a curated subset of geotechnical experiments (e.g., specific loading paths, boundary conditions, or soil types) hypothesized to produce distinct, critical deformation mechanisms. Digital Image Correlation (DIC) is then employed not just for general observation, but to acquire high-resolution, full-field displacement and strain data specifically from these targeted experiments. The rich DIC datasets are analyzed to extract quantitative signatures (e.g., strain localization patterns, evolution of shear bands, displacement discontinuities) that define the onset and progression of the hypothesized failure modes. This process creates a validated, data-rich corpus of 'mechanism exemplars' that can directly inform and constrain the development of next-generation constitutive models, prioritizing model fidelity for the most consequential physical behaviors observed in the selected experiments.

ideas.title: Full-Scale Prototype Validation Using a Biomedical Clinical Trial Framework
ideas.description: This idea adapts the phased validation framework from biomedical clinical trials to the problem of scaling up geotechnical centrifuge findings. In drug development, phased human trials (Phase I-III) systematically bridge the gap between promising lab results and real-world application. The adapted research idea applies this logic to coastal engineering: construct and instrument a full-scale breakwater section with seepage control not as a single test, but through a structured, multi-phase field trial. Phase I involves a single prototype cell under controlled conditions to validate core mechanics and instrumentation. Phase II tests a multi-cell section with design variations to assess performance and robustness. Phase III conducts a long-term, real-environment deployment to validate durability and effectiveness, thereby providing a rigorous, staged pathway from centrifuge model to proven, field-validated design.

ideas.title: Visualizing Pore Pressure Dynamics in Breakwater Cores Under Realistic Seismic Loading
ideas.description: This research direction combines the application of realistic, recorded earthquake ground motions to physical breakwater models with the use of medical imaging (e.g., fast MRI or CT) to visualize internal pore water flow in real-time. The core novelty is using the imaging technology to capture the transient, three-dimensional response of the pore fluid and soil skeleton to the complex frequency content and strong motion pulses of real seismic events. This allows researchers to directly identify which specific phases of an earthquake record (e.g., high-velocity pulses) are most critical for initiating internal erosion, excess pore pressure buildup, or liquefaction, moving beyond correlating failure to peak acceleration alone. The synergy creates a powerful diagnostic tool to validate and refine numerical models of seismic soil-fluid-structure interaction with unprecedented internal detail.

ideas.title: Microfluidic mapping of heterogeneous degradation pathways in rubble mound structures
ideas.description: This research direction uses purpose-designed microfluidic chips that replicate the complex pore networks of rubble mounds to experimentally investigate how spatially heterogeneous material properties drive failure. By fabricating chips with controlled zones of varying permeability, surface roughness, and particle size distributions—mimicking real-world degradation and non-uniform construction—we can visualize and measure pore-scale fluid-particle interactions and internal erosion dynamics. This allows for the development of constitutive models that directly connect measurable local heterogeneities to changes in global seepage forces and stability, moving beyond idealized uniform assumptions. The resulting high-resolution data will inform next-generation numerical models that can predict breakwater failure by simulating the progression of internal damage initiated at weak, heterogeneous zones.

ideas.title: Granular Seepage Erosion Reactor with Coupled Fluid-Particle Imaging
ideas.description: This approach studies fundamental fluid-particle interactions by creating a scaled, instrumented granular column that replicates the deformable and polydisperse nature of rubble. A pressure-controlled fluid flow is introduced, and the system uses synchronized high-resolution imaging (such as fast μCT or refractive-index-matched confocal microscopy) to simultaneously capture the evolving fluid phase and the displacement/rotation of individual grains in three dimensions. This allows for the direct measurement of coupled processes like particle entrainment, pore network evolution, and force chain redistribution during seepage. The result is a validated, physics-rich model that bridges the gap between idealized microfluidics and the complex reality of geophysical and planetary rubble.

ideas.title: Probabilistic Pore-Scale Forecasting for Seepage in Rubble
ideas.description: This research direction uses microfluidic chips designed as simplified, reproducible models of rubble pore networks to conduct high-throughput experiments on fluid-particle interactions. By applying ensemble methods and uncertainty quantification techniques from climate modeling—such as perturbing initial particle configurations, fluid properties, and chip geometries—we generate probabilistic libraries of pore-scale outcomes. This allows us to statistically upscale the microfluidic results to inform and validate continuum-scale seepage models, explicitly linking the uncertainty in fundamental processes to the confidence in facility-scale predictions. The synergy enables a new paradigm of 'probabilistic pore-scale forecasting' for managing seepage in critical infrastructures like landfill covers or dam filter zones.

ideas.title: Closed-Loop Adaptive Seepage Control to Prevent Internal Erosion
ideas.description: This research designs and tests a smart geotechnical system that actively manages, rather than blocks, seepage while guaranteeing the prevention of internal erosion. It employs a dense sensor array to continuously monitor the critical parameters of seepage flow and soil response in real time. An adaptive physical mechanism, such as a valve array or electrokinetic barrier, is then automatically adjusted based on this data to regulate flow rates and pathways, keeping them within empirically derived stability envelopes. The outcome is a robust methodology for safely dissipating pore pressure without risking the initiation of piping or other failure modes associated with uncontrolled seepage.

ideas.title: Replace idealized seismic testing with a 'motion library' approach adapted from cardiovascular flow research
ideas.description: This idea challenges the assumption that a simple 1 Hz ramped sine wave is sufficient for seismic testing. Drawing an analogy from cardiovascular engineering, where heart valve testing evolved from using simple sinusoidal pressure waves to libraries of realistic, patient-derived waveforms, this research proposes a parallel shift in earthquake engineering. The solution is to replace the specified idealized motion with a curated 'seismic motion library' containing recorded ground motions from historical earthquakes, categorized by key parameters like tectonic environment and site conditions. This adapted approach would subject test structures to the complex, non-sinusoidal, and multi-frequency content of real seismic events, providing a more rigorous and realistic basis for evaluating structural performance and code provisions.

ideas.title: Hazard-Consistent Seismic Testing Using Conditional Spectrum-Matched Record Selection and Scaling
ideas.description: This approach replaces the simple ramped sine wave with a suite of real earthquake ground motions rigorously selected and scaled to be consistent with a site-specific probabilistic seismic hazard analysis. Motions are chosen from a database to match a target conditional spectrum (CS) for a given intensity measure, such as spectral acceleration at the structure's fundamental period, ensuring they represent the frequency content and variability expected at the site. This method provides a reproducible, hazard-informed basis for testing, yielding results that are directly interpretable within a performance-based earthquake engineering framework.

ideas.title: Probabilistic Inversion for Physically Realistic Input Motions Constrained by Target Geotechnical Performance
ideas.description: This redesigned approach solves the ill-posed inverse problem by finding the ground motion that is most likely to occur, given established seismological principles, while still producing a specific slope displacement. It uses a physics-constrained generative adversarial network or a Bayesian framework to sample from the space of possible input motions, heavily penalizing unrealistic signals. The output is not a single motion but a family of probable motions and their associated likelihoods, all consistent with the target displacement. This provides a robust, physically meaningful answer for hazard analysis and engineering design.

ideas.title: Hypothesis-Driven Slope Gradients to Probe Locomotor and Stability Thresholds
ideas.description: This redesigned approach moves beyond arbitrary slope variation by first establishing a predictive biomechanical or ecological model, such as one based on energy cost or tipping-point stability. A gradient of slopes is then constructed to specifically test the predictions of this model, for example, by densely sampling angles around a predicted performance collapse point. The experiment measures key response variables (e.g., metabolic rate, gait kinematics, failure rate) to rigorously test the model. This transforms the experiment from a parameter sweep into a focused investigation that can validate a general principle of slope negotiation.

ideas.title: Inverse Filter Design for Target Structural Response
ideas.description: This idea adapts the solution to the inverse filter problem from signal processing to geotechnical earthquake engineering. The analogous problem is determining the input signal needed to produce a specific output from a known linear system, solved via deconvolution. Here, the 'system' is the slope, and its dynamic response to seismic loading must be characterized (e.g., via a transfer function from a simplified model or empirical data). The research idea is to formulate and solve the inverse problem: given a target slope displacement, compute the required input ground motion through deconvolution or an equivalent optimization framework, addressing practical constraints like motion realizability and system nonlinearities.

ideas.title: Bayesian Uncertainty-Quantifying Framework for Inverse Estimation of Soil Constitutive Parameters
ideas.description: This redesigned approach formulates the parameter estimation as a Bayesian inverse problem, where the goal is to infer posterior probability distributions for the constitutive parameters rather than point estimates. The framework integrates the physical model (the constitutive equations) directly into the inference process, ensuring all considered parameter combinations obey governing laws. By using Markov Chain Monte Carlo sampling, it produces full probabilistic outputs that explicitly show the uncertainty, correlation, and non-uniqueness in the estimated parameters, providing a robust and interpretable result from displacement and pore pressure data.

ideas.title: Dynamic risk trajectory warning for breakwater stability under cyclic loading
ideas.description: This system moves beyond a direct parameter transfer by developing a hybrid physics-based and data-driven early warning framework for breakwater failure. It continuously assimilates real-time data from in-situ pore pressure networks, wave conditions, and structural deformation sensors. A core computational model, trained on high-fidelity coupled numerical simulations and physical model tests, evaluates the breakwater's state against learned failure pathways under cyclic loading. The output is a time-evolving probabilistic risk index, providing actionable warnings based on the structure's dynamic trajectory toward instability rather than a static soil-derived threshold.

ideas.title: Causal Framework for Mechanistic Links Between Primary Geomechanical Response and Secondary Geophysical/Chemical Metrics
ideas.description: This research moves beyond exploratory correlation hunting by developing a physics-informed and hypothesis-driven framework. It posits specific mechanistic pathways (e.g., fracture dilation altering seismic velocity, fluid-rock interactions changing resistivity) through which primary geomechanical variables (displacement, pore pressure) govern secondary response metrics. The methodology employs causal discovery techniques and controlled numerical experiments to distinguish direct causal links from spurious correlations, ensuring identified relationships are robust and interpretable for improving monitoring and forecasting.

ideas.title: Adapting Seismic Aftershock Forecasting to Predict Breakwater Damage Cascades
ideas.description: This idea draws an analogy from seismology, where the Epidemic-Type Aftershock Sequence (ETAS) model is used to forecast aftershocks probabilistically based on the sequence of prior events. The analogous solution involves treating earthquake triggering as a self-exciting stochastic process. Adapted to breakwater stability, the research would develop a monitoring and modeling system that identifies initial, localized damage events within the breakwater structure. These are treated as triggering events. A statistical model, analogous to ETAS, would then be calibrated using historical failure data and physical simulations to forecast the probability and rate of subsequent, cascading damage events (e.g., progressive armor layer unraveling, core material washout). This transforms the early warning from a static pore pressure threshold to a dynamic, probabilistic forecast of failure progression, enabling more nuanced and timely maintenance or evacuation decisions.

ideas.title: Adapt Speckle Interferometry from Aerospace Composite Testing for High-Resolution Soil Deformation Mapping
ideas.description: This idea adapts a high-sensitivity optical measurement technique, Electronic Speckle Pattern Interferometry (ESPI), from the field of aerospace engineering, where it is used for non-destructive testing of composite materials to detect micron-scale defects and strain concentrations. The analogous solution involves using laser interference patterns to compute full-field displacements with extreme precision. Adapted to geotechnical research, ESPI would be employed in laboratory settings on speckle-patterned soil specimens or physical models. By analyzing the phase shifts in laser speckle patterns before and during loading, researchers could map full-field soil deformation with sub-micron resolution, enabling the study of incipient failure mechanisms like shear band initiation and micro-crack propagation that precede bulk soil failure, offering a powerful new tool for fundamental soil mechanics investigation.
